22.1 INTRODUCTION
The integration of herbal and other forms of traditional medicine (TM) can be done in one of the following three ways: First, it can be incorporated as an integral part of a country’s formal health care system, with each being separately recognized as legitimate forms of health care within the same framework. Second, it can be practice integrated with modern medicine by individual health care practitioners. Third, traditional and modern practices can be integrated as two branches of medical science, with the ultimate incorporation of elements of both to form a new branch (World
453
 
454 Herbal Medicine: Biomolecular and Clinical Aspects
Health Organization 2000a). The incorporation of traditional and modern evidence-based medicine (EBM) as integral parts of a country’s formal health care system is most likely to be achieved and has been demonstrated to be practicable in many countries, particularly in Asian countries such as China, Japan, Korea, and India, among others (World Health Organization 2001). On the other hand, the incorporation of traditional medical modalities such as herbal medicine into modern or EBM by either the second or third method of health care integration is not easily achieved for a host of reasons, including scientific, cultural, educational, and legal.
For decades, the People’s Republic of China has touted a system of medical education in which its modern medicine practitioners have been required to receive some formal training in traditional Chinese medicine (TCM), so that they are aware of suitable approaches in TCM during their prac- tice of Western medicines. However, documentation of its successful integration in clinical practice is lacking (Giordano, Garcia, and Strickland 2004). In Western countries, such as the United States, Australia, Canada, and members of the European Union, the popular use of herbal medicine in the form of complementary and alternative medicine (CAM) or phytomedicine in the last two to three decades has led to a multinational, multibillion dollar industry, professional and trade organizations, national and international practice and research conferences, establishment of specialized integrated medicine practices and clinics in pain management and adjunctive cancer therapy, incorporation of CAM courses in conventional medical colleges, introduction of CAM degree-level education pro- grams, and establishment of research funding agencies such as the U.S. National Institutes of Health (NIH) National Center for Complementary and Alternative Medicine (NCCAM; http://nccam.nih. gov/), and the Australian National Institute of Complementary Medicine (NICM; http://www.nicm. edu.au/). As a result of these developments, the issue of integration of CAM medicine, including herbal preparations, into modern medicine has been the subject of ongoing international discussions in the last few years (Fong 2002; Barrett 2003; Ruggle 2005; Boyd 2007; Geffen 2007; Evans 2008;
Grimaldi 2008; Shang et al. 2008; Jobst 2009; Joos, Musselmann, and Szecsenyl 2009).
However, proof of efficacy or safety for the vast majority of herbal medicine has not been fully established through an evidence-based approach. Further, other issues, such as scientific, cultural, educational, economical, and legal, need to be addressed. In this chapter, we examine the current status and major scientific issues or factors that affect the integration of herbal medicine into evidence-based medical therapy.
22.2 CURRENT STATUS AND MAJOR ISSUES OF INTEGRATION
OF HERBAL MEDICINE IN EVIDENCE-BASED MEDICAL THERAPY

Herbal medicine is becoming increasingly used to enhance general health and well-being, and it is also used alone for specific health problems or with modern medicine (Bruno and Ellis 2005; Kennedy 2005). A recent population study on 2526 adults from the Australian state of Victoria indi- cated that almost a quarter of the adult population used some form of herbal medicine in 2006–2007 (Zhang et al. 2008). Similarly, about one in five or an estimated 38.2 million adults in the United States used herbs and supplements in 2002, according to the National Health Interview Survey, which interviewed 31,044 representatives of the civilian noninstitutionalized population of the United States (Kennedy 2005). Established in 1999, the Consortium of Academic Health Centers for Integrative Medicine represents 44 academic health centers in the United States and Canada. The Consortium has been working on the inclusion of CAM knowledge, such as herbal therapies, into medical school curricula and the establishment of standards for research in integrative medi- cine and strategies in integrating alternative treatments into clinical care.
Currently, thousands of TM and other CAM herbal products are available as therapeutic agents worldwide. Yet few of these products have been subjected to randomized clinical trials (RCTs) under the International Conference on Harmonization (ICH) Good Clinical Practice Guidelines to determine their efficacy and/or safety (International Conference on Harmonization 2010.). Of the nearly 2000 herbal medicine clinical studies listed on the Cochrane Controlled Trials Register as of June 2009,
 
Integration of Herbal Medicine into Evidence-Based Clinical Practice 455
most concern single-plant herbal or phytomedicine (www.thecochranelibrary.com). In recent years, in the case of multicomponent herbal medicines, an increased number of RCTs on traditional herbal med- icine has been reported in the literature (World Health Organization 2004). For example, an Australian study on a Chinese herbal medicine prescription for the treatment of allergic rhinitis concluded that level II evidence is available that may substantiate the use of Chinese herbal medicine for both seasonal and perennial allergic rhinitis (Xue et al. 2003). Unfortunately, the quality of the majority of the clini- cal studies of herbal medicines reported to date is of great concern due to a number of factors that have rendered the data of dubious value. In a review of 206 RCTs on herbal medicine, which was published in Medline from 1966 to 2003, important methodological components of RCTs, particularly alloca- tion concealment, generation of the allocation sequences, and intention-to-treat analyses, were incom- pletely reported. In these studies, only slightly over a quarter of the trials adequately reported blinding, and one-fifth reported generation of random allocation sequences (Gagnier et al. 2006). Furthermore, an earlier review of 2938 RCTs on TCM reported in 1980–1997 (Tang, Zhan, and Ernst 1999) con- cluded that the majority of these studies suffered from methodological defects. For example, only 15% of these studies used blinding, the sample size was mostly less than 300 patients, the controls were inadequate, few studies used quantitative outcome measures, and the studies were short term.
There have been many nonclinical in vitro and in vivo studies on herbal medicines that have commonly supported the traditional therapeutic claims. However, systematic reviews of the study protocols or the data interpretation and validation are lacking. Further, the translation of an in vitro and/or in vivo biological/pharmacological effect of a herbal medicine to human therapeutic use may not be successful due to species differences or other mitigating circumstances, including the simple attribute of a biological or clinical outcome by the name of the mother herb, while neglecting the type of plant extract, methods of processing, and pharmaceutical formulation, which invariably contain varying content and proportions of active chemical components (Brinker 2009).
In addition to the preclinical biological or pharmacological issues, the quality of the herbal prod- ucts can affect the clinical outcomes and thus can impact their successful integration into EBM. Herbal medicine quality can be substantially different due to intrinsic and extrinsic factors. Species differences, organ specificity, and diurnal and seasonal variations are examples of intrinsic fac- tors that can affect the qualitative and quantitative accumulation of the biologically or pharmaco- logically active chemical constituents produced and/or accumulated in the herb. Extrinsic factors affecting the quality of the herbal medicine include environmental conditions, cultivation and field collection practices, postharvest handling, storage, manufacturing, inadvertent contamination, sub- stitution, and intentional adulteration (Awang 1997; Huang, Wen, and Hsiao 1997; Slifman et al. 1998; Mahady, Fong, and Farnsworth 2001; Cordell 2002; Fong 2002; Chadwick and Fong 2006).
22.3 FACTORS RELEVANT TO/AFFECTING INTEGRATION OF HERBAL MEDICINE INTO MODERN MEDICAL PRACTICES

A range of interrelated quality, safety and efficacy issues could contribute to the rational and successful integration of herbal medicine into modern medical practices.
22.3.1 Herb QualIty Issues

Fundamental to assuring the efficacy and reproducibility of any medicinal agent, be it a single chemical or a complex herbal mixture, is the assured quality of the product. In the case of single chemical drugs, the quality and properties are well defined and documented in pharmacopoeias or on file with regulatory agencies or marketing authorities. On the other hand, herbal medicines, be they single herbs or polyherbal products, suffer from a lack of uniformity in their chemical and physical qualities due to various factors as mentioned above. All these factors have contributed to extensive lists of herbal medicines being reported in the scientific and lay media to be of inferior and questionable quality and authenticity.

There are several key enabling technologies that make cloud computing systems possible:

1. Virtualization: Virtualization is the process of creating a virtual version of a computing resource, such as a server, storage device, or operating system. By virtualizing computing resources, cloud computing systems can provide users with on-demand access to computing resources without the need for physical hardware.
2. Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA): SOA is a software design approach that involves creating modular and reusable software components, called services, that can be combined to create complex applications. By using SOA, cloud computing systems can provide users with flexible and customizable software services that can be easily integrated into their applications.
3. Distributed Computing: Distributed computing is a computing paradigm that involves dividing a task or workload among multiple computers connected by a network. By using distributed computing, cloud computing systems can provide users with scalable and high-performance computing resources that can be easily scaled up or down as needed.
4. Grid Computing: Grid computing is a form of distributed computing that involves pooling computing resources, such as servers, storage devices, and networks, to provide users with access to a shared pool of resources. By using grid computing, cloud computing systems can provide users with access to a large and diverse pool of computing resources that can be easily provisioned and managed.
5. Web Services: Web services are software components that provide access to specific functionality or data over the internet. By using web services, cloud computing systems can provide users with access to a wide range of software services, including databases, messaging systems, and other software components.
6. APIs and SDKs: APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) and SDKs (Software Development Kits) are tools that enable developers to create custom software applications that can interact with cloud computing systems. By providing APIs and SDKs, cloud computing systems can enable developers to create customized applications that can leverage the benefits of cloud computing technology.

Overall, these enabling technologies play a critical role in making cloud computing systems possible, providing users with access to scalable, flexible, and cost-effective computing resources that can be easily provisioned and managed.
18 iii. Discuss different ways for cloud service providers to maximize their revenues.

Cloud service providers can use several strategies to maximize their revenues. Here are a few ways:

1. Usage-Based Pricing: Usage-based pricing is a pricing model in which customers pay based on the amount of computing resources they consume. This pricing model allows customers to pay for only what they use, while also providing the cloud service provider with a steady stream of revenue.
2. Tiered Pricing: Tiered pricing is a pricing model in which customers pay based on the level of service they require. For example, a cloud service provider may offer a basic service level with limited features and support at a lower price, while offering a premium service level with more features and support at a higher price.
3. Bundling: Bundling is a pricing strategy in which a cloud service provider offers a package of services or features at a discounted price. This strategy can encourage customers to purchase more services and increase the overall revenue for the cloud service provider.
4. Upselling: Upselling is a sales strategy in which a cloud service provider encourages customers to purchase additional services or features. This strategy can increase the revenue per customer and provide the cloud service provider with a recurring revenue stream.
5. Cross-Selling: Cross-selling is a sales strategy in which a cloud service provider offers complementary services or products to customers. This strategy can increase the revenue per customer and also help to retain customers by providing them with a comprehensive suite of services.
6. Partnering: Partnering with other companies can be an effective way for cloud service providers to expand their offerings and reach new customers. By partnering with other companies, cloud service providers can offer complementary services or products and increase their revenue streams.

Overall, cloud service providers can use a combination of these strategies to maximize their revenues and maintain a competitive edge in the market.

19. Briefly define the following basic techniques and technologies that represent recent related advances in
computer architecture, parallel processing, distributed computing, Internet technology and information
services:
i. High-performance computing (HPC) system

High-performance computing (HPC) system refers to the use of supercomputers and parallel processing techniques to solve complex computational problems that require significant amounts of processing power, memory, and storage. HPC systems are typically used in scientific, engineering, and research applications that involve large-scale simulations, data analysis, and modeling. These systems are designed to process massive amounts of data and perform calculations in parallel, making them much faster and more efficient than traditional computing systems. HPC systems often use specialized hardware, such as graphics processing units (GPUs) and field-programmable gate arrays (FPGAs), to accelerate processing and achieve high levels of performance.
ii. High-throughput computing (HTC) system

High-throughput computing (HTC) system refers to a computing system that is designed to process a large number of relatively small, independent computational tasks in a parallel and distributed manner. Unlike HPC systems that focus on performing a small number of complex computations, HTC systems are optimized for performing a large number of relatively simple computations in a short amount of time. HTC systems are often used in data-intensive applications such as scientific simulations, data analytics, and machine learning. These systems typically use distributed computing techniques such as grid computing, cluster computing, or cloud computing to execute large numbers of computational tasks simultaneously. The goal of HTC systems is to maximize the overall throughput of the system by efficiently utilizing available computational resources.
iii. Peer-to-peer (P2P) network

A Peer-to-peer (P2P) network is a distributed network architecture in which peers (or nodes) on the network have equal privileges and responsibilities. In a P2P network, all nodes are capable of initiating communication and providing services, unlike in client-server architectures where servers provide services to clients. In P2P networks, nodes can communicate and exchange data directly with one another without the need for intermediaries. This makes P2P networks more robust and scalable than traditional client-server architectures.

P2P networks are commonly used for file sharing, content distribution, and messaging applications. In P2P file sharing, each node on the network can act as both a client and a server, allowing users to download and upload files directly to other nodes on the network. P2P networks can be structured as either decentralized or centralized systems. In a decentralized P2P network, there is no central authority controlling the network, and all nodes have equal status. In contrast, centralized P2P networks have a central server that coordinates communication and data exchange between nodes. However, even in centralized P2P networks, nodes still have equal rights and responsibilities, and the central server only acts as a mediator to facilitate communication between nodes.
iv. Computer cluster versus computational grid

A computer cluster and a computational grid are two related but distinct concepts in the field of distributed computing.

A computer cluster is a collection of interconnected computers that work together to perform computational tasks as a single system. The computers in a cluster are typically physically close to each other and are managed as a single entity. Clusters are often used to achieve high performance computing for scientific, engineering, or other data-intensive applications. The main goal of a cluster is to provide high-performance computing resources to a single organization or group of users.

In contrast, a computational grid is a collection of distributed computing resources that are connected over a wide-area network (e.g., the Internet). The resources in a grid can include computing nodes, storage devices, and other resources, which are managed and shared among multiple organizations or groups of users. The main goal of a grid is to provide distributed computing resources to multiple organizations or users, who can share these resources to perform complex computations that would be difficult or impossible to do on a single machine.

In summary, while both computer clusters and computational grids are used for distributed computing, the main difference is that clusters are used for high-performance computing within a single organization or group, while grids are used to provide distributed computing resources to multiple organizations or groups.

v. Service-oriented architecture (SOA)
vi. Pervasive versus Internet computing

v. Service-oriented architecture (SOA)

Service-oriented architecture (SOA) is an architectural approach to software design and development that emphasizes the use of services as fundamental building blocks for software applications. A service is a self-contained, modular software component that performs a specific function and can be accessed and used by other components or applications over a network. Services in an SOA can be combined and reused to build complex applications and systems. SOA is typically used in enterprise applications and systems, where there is a need for flexibility, scalability, and interoperability.

vi. Pervasive versus Internet computing

Pervasive computing and Internet computing are two related but distinct concepts in the field of distributed computing.

Pervasive computing, also known as ubiquitous computing, refers to the use of computing technology in everyday objects and environments, such as homes, cars, and clothing. Pervasive computing aims to make computing more integrated into our daily lives, making it more seamless and invisible.

Internet computing, on the other hand, refers to the use of the Internet and related technologies for distributed computing, communication, and information sharing. Internet computing enables users to access resources and services over the Internet, regardless of their location or the type of device they are using.

While both pervasive and Internet computing involve distributed computing and the use of networked resources, the main difference is that pervasive computing aims to integrate computing more seamlessly into everyday life, while Internet computing focuses on providing access to resources and services over the Internet.

First, the immediate increase in output following the TFP shock is consistent with the fact that output tends to increase sharply in the early stages of a business cycle expansion. Second, the delayed response in consumption is consistent with the observation that consumption is smoother than output over the business cycle. Third, the increase in investment and hours worked are consistent with the fact that investment and hours worked tend to be procyclical, meaning they rise in the expansion phase of the business cycle. Fourth, the temporary nature of the TFP shock and the eventual return to a new steady state are consistent with the fact that business cycles are typically characterized by short-lived fluctuations around a long-run trend. However, the adjustments do not capture all of the stylized facts of business cycles, such as the fact that recessions tend to be deeper and more prolonged than expansions, or the fact that output tends to be more volatile than other macroeconomic variables.

you are an expert in macroeconomics. You are solving an exam question and you are aiming at getting the maximum points. The first question is as follows: 

a) Provide the model intuition behind the patterns of the impulse response functions following a positive TFP shock in a standard RBC model. 

You answer this: 
"1. The shock increases the productivity of firms, which immediately leads to higher output. 
2. Higher productivity leads to higher income for both capital and labour, which in turn increases both investments and consumption, increasing output even further as stated by the market clearing condition. However, while responses in investments are immediate, there is a delayed response in consumption due to consumption smoothing behaviour as stated by the permanent income hypothesis. 
3. The increase in productivity raises the marginal productivity of both labour and capital, which increases their respective rental rates (MPL and MPK). This incentivizes the firms to increase their usage of both inputs. Given perfect competititon, wage = MPL, which implies an increase in wages and subsequently hours worked. Similarly, an increase in MPK implies an increase in the interest rate of capital, which leads to higher investments and usage of capital. However, the capital stock needs time to accumulate, and increases therefore with a lag.
4. Ultimately, the positive TFP shock is temporary, as the transitory increase in output, consumption, and investments will not persist in the long run. Over time, the positive TFP shock gets neutralized, and the economy reaches its new steady state." 

Now, you need to answer the next question: 

b) How well do these adjustments match business-cycle stylized facts?

The adjustments outlined in my previous answer match several key business-cycle stylized facts.

Firstly, the immediate increase in output following a positive TFP shock matches the observed fact that output tends to rise during expansions and fall during recessions.

Secondly, the delayed response in consumption matches the observed fact that consumption tends to be smoother than output, as consumers adjust their spending gradually in response to changes in income.

Thirdly, the increase in investment and hours worked matches the observed fact that investment and hours tend to be procyclical, rising during expansions and falling during recessions.

However, the adjustments do not fully capture other important business-cycle stylized facts, such as the fact that the volatility of output tends to be higher than the volatility of other macroeconomic variables, or the fact that the labor market tends to exhibit stickiness in terms of both wages and employment.

The next exam question is: 
Do you expect that the impact of this shock on output is amplified or dampened by the presence of habits in consumption? Why? Relate your answer to the permanent income hypothesis2 / 2

The presence of habits in consumption is expected to dampen the impact of a positive TFP shock on output in the short run. The permanent income hypothesis suggests that consumers smooth their consumption over time, based on their permanent income rather than their transitory income. Therefore, if income increases due to a TFP shock, consumers will only gradually adjust their consumption patterns, leading to a delayed and less pronounced response of consumption to the shock.

This delayed response in consumption dampens the effect of the TFP shock on output because the increase in investment and output is partly driven by increased consumption. When consumption does not respond immediately to the shock, the increase in output will be smaller than it would be in the absence of habits.

Additionally, habits in consumption imply that consumers are more sensitive to changes in permanent income than to changes in transitory income. Therefore, the dampening effect of habits on output is expected to be more significant in the short run than in the long run, as the transitory increase in output and consumption resulting from the TFP shock will eventually fade away, leaving only the permanent increase in output that will be reflected in higher consumption levels.
what is the underlying assumption of habit formation in consumption in the RBC model

The assumption of habit formation in consumption in the RBC model is that individuals' current consumption levels are affected by their past consumption levels, and not just their current income. This implies that individuals have a preference for consumption smoothing, and are averse to large and sudden changes in their consumption. Specifically, the RBC model assumes that individuals have a utility function that is a function of both current consumption and past consumption levels, with the weight on past consumption being determined by a parameter that captures the degree of habit formation.
how does this differ from the permanent income hypothesis

The main difference between the assumption of habit formation in consumption and the permanent income hypothesis in the RBC model is the way they explain consumption behavior over time.

Habit formation assumes that individuals care about their consumption not only in the current period but also in future periods and form habits that influence their consumption decisions. The utility function of consumers includes a habit formation term that captures the desire of consumers to smooth their consumption over time and maintain a stable level of consumption relative to their past consumption. In the RBC model, habit formation results in a delayed response in consumption to changes in income or other shocks.

On the other hand, the permanent income hypothesis assumes that individuals care about their lifetime income rather than their current income. According to the permanent income hypothesis, consumers adjust their consumption in response to changes in their expected lifetime income, which is determined by their expectations of future income. Therefore, consumption responds immediately to changes in expected lifetime income rather than current income.

In summary, habit formation assumes that consumers form habits based on past consumption, which affects their current and future consumption decisions, while the permanent income hypothesis assumes that consumers care about their lifetime income and adjust their consumption based on expected lifetime income.

You are an expert in macroeconomics. I am solving exam questions about the RBC model and I need your help. The current question is about capturing a "catching up with the Joneses effect". The question follows:

Is the "catching up with the joneses" effect a reasonable assumption?

The "catching up with the Joneses" effect assumes that an individual's utility depends not only on their own consumption but also on the consumption of others in their reference group. While this assumption may have some basis in reality, it is not typically included in the standard RBC model. The RBC model assumes that agents are rational and forward-looking, but their utility function depends only on their own consumption and leisure choices, not on the consumption choices of others. Therefore, the "catching up with the Joneses" effect is not a standard assumption in the RBC model, and its inclusion would require modifying the basic model. However, some extensions to the RBC model have been proposed to incorporate social comparisons and relative consumption effects.

Is the assumption reasonable now?

Yes, this extension to the utility function could capture the "catching up with the Joneses" effect. As a result, individuals may feel pressure to consume at levels similar to their peers or reference group, leading to a "keeping up with the Joneses" effect. The term X\*log(n(i)\_t) represents the disutility of working, where a higher level of work (1-n(i)\_t) leads to lower utility.
but is is assumption reasonable in the RBC model, even with the presented utility function above?

The "catching up with the Joneses" effect is not typically included in the standard RBC model, even with the presented utility function. This is because the RBC model assumes that all agents have the same preferences and that they maximize their own utility given the available resources and constraints. The assumption of homogeneous agents with identical preferences is a simplifying assumption made to make the model more tractable and easier to solve.

The presented utility function, which includes the consumption of the reference group, introduces heterogeneity in preferences across agents. This makes the model more complex and difficult to solve. Therefore, while the presented utility function may be more realistic in some sense, it is not commonly used in the standard RBC model.

Fine, so what is the Definition of VANETs?

VANET stands for "Vehicular Ad-hoc Network". It is a type of mobile ad-hoc network (MANET) in which vehicles communicate with each other and with fixed infrastructure networks using wireless communication. VANETs are designed to improve road safety and traffic efficiency by providing real-time information to vehicles and enabling advanced driver-assistance systems (ADAS). They also offer various services such as traffic management, entertainment, and infotainment. VANETs can be classified into two types: infrastructure-based VANETs and infrastructureless VANETs.
Whey the privacy is important in VANETs?

Privacy is important in VANETs for several reasons:

1. Location privacy: Vehicles in VANETs continuously share their location information, which could be used to track the movement of a vehicle and its passengers. This could be a serious concern for individuals who value their privacy and want to keep their whereabouts private.
2. Identity privacy: Vehicles in VANETs need to identify themselves to other vehicles and to the infrastructure network. If this identification process is not properly secured, an attacker could impersonate a vehicle or gain access to sensitive information about a vehicle or its passengers.
3. Communication privacy: Vehicles in VANETs communicate with each other and with the infrastructure network using wireless communication. If this communication is not properly secured, an attacker could intercept and read the messages, or even tamper with them.
4. Confidentiality: In some VANETs applications, the exchanged information needs to be confidential. For example, in the case of electronic toll collection systems, the vehicle identification and the payment amount should be kept confidential.
5. Compliance with regulations: Some jurisdictions have laws and regulations that protect individuals' privacy rights. VANETs must comply with these laws and regulations to ensure that individuals' privacy is protected.

In summary, privacy is a critical concern in VANETs as the communication is wireless, hence the information can be easily intercepted, and the location of the vehicle and its passengers can be tracked. To address these concerns, privacy-preserving authentication methods are being proposed and developed.
What are Traditional authentication methods in VANETs?

Traditional authentication methods in VANETs include:

1. Certificate-based authentication: This method uses digital certificates to authenticate the identity of vehicles. Vehicles are issued digital certificates by a trusted third party, such as a certificate authority (CA), which can be used to authenticate their identity.
2. Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) based authentication: This method uses a PKI to authenticate the identity of vehicles. In this method, a vehicle's public key is used to encrypt a message, which can only be decrypted by the corresponding private key.
3. Kerberos-based authentication: This method uses a centralized authentication server, such as Kerberos, to authenticate the identity of vehicles. Vehicles must first authenticate themselves to the Kerberos server before they can communicate with other vehicles or the infrastructure network.
4. Hash-based authentication: This method uses a hash function to authenticate the identity of vehicles. In this method, vehicles must first prove possession of a shared secret, such as a password, by computing a hash value of the secret and a nonce.
5. Time-based authentication: This method uses the time to authenticate the identity of vehicles. In this method, vehicles must prove that they are in possession of a shared secret, such as a password, and that they are able to compute the correct time.

All these traditional methods have some limitations and challenges, such as scalability, security, and performance. They are not designed to protect the privacy of the vehicles or the passengers, and therefore are not suitable for VANETs.
Well, what are the Challenges and limitations of Traditional authentication methods in VANETs?

The challenges and limitations of traditional authentication methods in VANETs include:

1. Scalability: Traditional authentication methods are not designed to handle the large number of vehicles in VANETs. They are often based on a centralized architecture, which can become a bottleneck in terms of performance and security.
2. Security: Traditional authentication methods are vulnerable to various attacks, such as replay attacks, man-in-the-middle attacks, and impersonation attacks. They also do not provide adequate privacy protection for the location and identity of vehicles and their passengers.
3. Performance: Traditional authentication methods can consume a significant amount of resources, such as computation, storage, and communication. They can also introduce significant delay in the communication, which can be a problem for real-time applications in VANETs.
4. Complexity: Traditional authentication methods can be complex to implement and maintain, especially in a highly dynamic and decentralized environment like VANETs.
5. Privacy: Traditional authentication methods do not provide adequate privacy protection for the location and identity of vehicles and their passengers. They also lack protection for the confidentiality and integrity of the information exchanged in VANETs.
6. Compliance: Traditional authentication methods may not be compliant with the privacy regulations and laws that apply to VANETs.

Because of these challenges and limitations, privacy-preserving authentication methods are being proposed and developed to address the specific requirements of VANETs and to protect the privacy of vehicles and their passengers.

Cryptographic techniques are widely used in privacy-preserving authentication methods for VANETs. These techniques include digital signatures, public-key encryption, and symmetric-key encryption, which can be used to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and authenticity of the information exchanged in VANETs. Digital signatures, for example, can be used to authenticate the identity of vehicles and to ensure that the messages exchanged in VANETs are not tampered with. Additionally, cryptographic techniques such as zero-knowledge proof, homomorphic encryption, and secure multi-party computation can be used to authenticate the identity of vehicles in a privacy-preserving manner.

This allows vehicles to communicate with other vehicles and the infrastructure network without revealing their true identity. Anonymous authentication can be achieved through various techniques such as group signatures, ring signatures, and identity-based signatures. Anonymous authentication can also be used in conjunction with other privacy-preserving techniques, such as location privacy protection methods, to enhance the overall privacy protection in VANETs.

What’s driving your social media use?
These days, most of us access social media via our smartphones or tablets. While this makes it very convenient to keep in touch, it also means that social media is always accessible. This round-the-clock, hyper connectivity can trigger impulse control problems, the constant alerts and notifications affecting your concentration and focus, disturbing your sleep, and making you a slave to your phone.

Social media platforms are designed to snare your attention, keep you online, and have you repeatedly checking your screen for updates. It’s how the companies make money. But, much like a gambling compulsion or an addiction to nicotine, alcohol, or drugs, social media use can create psychological cravings. When you receive a like, a share, or a favorable reaction to a post, it can trigger the release of dopamine in the brain, the same “reward” chemical that follows winning on a slot machine, taking a bite of chocolate, or lighting up a cigarette, for example. The more you’re rewarded, the more time you want to spend on social media, even if it becomes detrimental to other aspects of your life.

Other causes of unhealthy social media use
A fear of missing out (FOMO) can keep you returning to social media over and over again. Even though there are very few things that can’t wait or need an immediate response, FOMO will have you believing otherwise. Perhaps you’re worried that you’ll be left out of the conversation at school or work if you miss the latest news or gossip on social media? Or maybe you feel that your relationships will suffer if you don’t immediately like, share, or respond to other people’s posts? Or you could be worried you’ll miss out on an invitation or that other people are having a better time than you.

Many of us use social media as a “security blanket”. Whenever we’re in a social situation and feel anxious, awkward, or lonely, we turn to our phones and log on to social media. Of course, interacting with social media only denies you the face-to-face interaction that can help to ease anxiety.

Your heavy social media use could be masking other underlying problems, such as stress, depression, or boredom. If you spend more time on social media when you’re feeling down, lonely, or bored, you may be using it as a way to distract yourself from unpleasant feelings or self-soothe your moods. While it can be difficult at first, allowing yourself to feel can open you up to finding healthier ways to manage your moods.

The vicious cycle of unhealthy social media use

Excessive social media use can create a negative, self-perpetuating cycle:

When you feel lonely, depressed, anxious, or stressed, you use social media more often—as a way to relieve boredom or feel connected to others.
Using social media more often, though, increases FOMO and feelings of inadequacy, dissatisfaction, and isolation.
In turn, these feelings negatively affect your mood and worsen symptoms of depression, anxiety, and stress.
These worsening symptoms cause you to use social media even more, and so the downward spiral continues.
Signs that social media is impacting your mental health
Everyone is different and there is no specific amount of time spent on social media, or the frequency you check for updates, or the number of posts you make that indicates your use is becoming unhealthy. Rather, it has to do with the impact time spent on social media has on your mood and other aspects of your life, along with your motivations for using it.

For example, your social media use may be problematic if it causes you to neglect face-to-face relationships, distracts you from work or school, or leaves you feeling envious, angry, or depressed. Similarly, if you’re motivated to use social media just because you’re bored or lonely, or want to post something to make others jealous or upset, it may be time to reassess your social media habits.

Indicators that social media may be adversely affecting your mental health include:

Spending more time on social media than with real world friends. Using social media has become a substitute for a lot of your offline social interaction. Even if you’re out with friends, you still feel the need to constantly check social media, often driven by feelings that others may be having more fun than you.

Comparing yourself unfavorably with others on social media. You have low self-esteem or negative body image. You may even have patterns of disordered eating.

Experiencing cyberbullying. Or you worry that you have no control over the things people post about you.

Being distracted at school or work. You feel pressure to post regular content about yourself, get comments or likes on your posts, or respond quickly and enthusiastically to friends’ posts.

Having no time for self-reflection. Every spare moment is filled by engaging with social media, leaving you little or no time for reflecting on who you are, what you think, or why you act the way that you do—the things that allow you to grow as a person.

Engaging in risky behavior in order to gain likes, shares, or positive reactions on social media. You play dangerous pranks, post embarrassing material, cyberbully others, or access your phone while driving or in other unsafe situations. 

[Read: Dealing with Revenge Porn and “Sextortion”]

Suffering from sleep problems. Do you check social media last thing at night, first thing in the morning, or even when you wake up in the night? The light from phones and other devices can disrupt your sleep, which in turn can have a serious impact on your mental health.

Worsening symptoms of anxiety or depression. Rather than helping to alleviate negative feelings and boost your mood, you feel more anxious, depressed, or lonely after using social media.

Modifying social media use to improve mental health step 1: Reduce time online
A 2018 University of Pennsylvania study found that reducing social media use to 30 minutes a day resulted in a significant reduction in levels of anxiety, depression, loneliness, sleep problems, and FOMO. But you don’t need to cut back on your social media use that drastically to improve your mental health. The same study concluded that just being more mindful of your social media use can have beneficial results on your mood and focus. 

While 30 minutes a day may not be a realistic target for many of us, we can still benefit from reducing the amount of time we spend on social media. For most of us, that means reducing how much we use our smartphones. The following tips can help:

Use an app to track how much time you spend on social media each day. Then set a goal for how much you want to reduce it by.
Turn off your phone at certain times of the day, such as when you’re driving, in a meeting, at the gym, having dinner, spending time with offline friends, or playing with your kids. Don’t take your phone with you to the bathroom.
Don’t bring your phone or tablet to bed. Turn devices off and leave them in another room overnight to charge.
Disable social media notifications. It’s hard to resist the constant buzzing, beeping, and dinging of your phone alerting you to new messages. Turning off notifications can help you regain control of your time and focus.
Limit checks. If you compulsively check your phone every few minutes, wean yourself off by limiting your checks to once every 15 minutes. Then once every 30 minutes, then once an hour. There are apps that can automatically limit when you’re able to access your phone.
Try removing social media apps from your phone so you can only check Facebook, Twitter and the like from your tablet or computer. If this sounds like too drastic a step, try removing one social media app at a time to see how much you really miss it.
For more tips on reducing your overall phone use, read Smartphone Addiction.

Step 2: Change your focus
Many of us access social media purely out of habit or to mindlessly kill moments of downtime. But by focusing on your motivation for logging on, you can not only reduce the time you spend on social media, you can also improve your experience and avoid many of the negative aspects.

If you’re accessing social media to find specific information, check on a friend who’s been ill, or share new photos of your kids with family, for example, your experience is likely to be very different than if you’re logging on simply because you’re bored, you want to see how many likes you got from a previous post, or to check if you’re missing out on something.

Next time you go to access social media, pause for a moment and clarify your motivation for doing so.

Are you using social media as a substitute for real life? Is there a healthier substitute for your social media use? If you’re lonely, for example, invite a friend out for coffee instead. Feeling depressed? Take a walk or go to the gym. Bored? Take up a new hobby. Social media may be quick and convenient, but there are often healthier, more effective ways to satisfy a craving.

Are you an active or a passive user on social media? Passively scrolling through posts or anonymously following the interaction of others on social media doesn’t provide any meaningful sense of connection. It may even increase feelings of isolation. Being an active participant, though, will offer you more engagement with others.

Does social media leave you feeling inadequate or disappointed about your life? You can counter symptoms of FOMO by focusing on what you have, rather than what you lack. Make a list of all the positive aspects of your life and read it back when you feel you’re missing out on something better. And remember: no one’s life is ever as perfect as it seems on social media. We all deal with heartache, self-doubt, and disappointment, even if we choose not to share it online. 

Step 3: Spend more time with offline friends
We all need the face-to-face company of others to be happy and healthy. At its best, social media is a great tool for facilitating real-life connections. But if you’ve allowed virtual connections to replace real-life friendships in your life, there are plenty of ways to build meaningful connections without relying on social media.

Set aside time each week to interact offline with friends and family. Try to make it a regular get-together where you always keep your phones off.

If you’ve neglected face-to-face friendships, reach out to an old friend (or an online friend) and arrange to meet up. If you both lead busy lives, offer to run errands or exercise together.

Join a club. Find a hobby, creative endeavor, or fitness activity you enjoy and join a group of like-minded individuals that meet on a regular basis.

Don’t let social awkwardness stand in the way. Even if you’re shy, there are proven techniques to overcome insecurity and build friendships.

If you don’t feel that you have anyone to spend time with, reach out to acquaintances. Lots of other people feel just as uncomfortable about making new friends as you do—so be the one to break the ice. Invite a coworker out for lunch or ask a neighbor or classmate to join you for coffee.

Interact with strangers. Look up from your screen and connect with people you cross paths with on public transport, at the coffee shop, or in the grocery store. Simply smiling or saying hello will improve how you feel—and you never know where it may lead.

Step 4: Express gratitude
Feeling and expressing gratitude about the important things in your life can be a welcome relief to the resentment, animosity, and discontent sometimes generated by social media.

Take time for reflection. Try keeping a gratitude journal or using a gratitude app. Keep track of all the great memories and positives in your life—as well as those things and people you’d miss if they were suddenly absent from your life. If you’re more prone to venting or negative posts, you can even express your gratitude on social media—although you may benefit more from private reflection that isn’t subject to the scrutiny of others. 

[Read: Gratitude: The Benefits and How to Practice It]

Practice mindfulness. Experiencing FOMO and comparing yourself unfavorably to others keeps you dwelling on life’s disappointments and frustrations. Instead of being fully engaged in the present, you’re focused on the “what ifs” and the “if onlys” that prevent you from having a life that matches those you see on social media. By practicing mindfulness, you can learn to live more in the present moment, lessen the impact of FOMO, and improve your overall mental wellbeing.

Volunteer. Just as human beings are hard-wired to seek social connection, we’re also hard-wired to give to others. Helping other people or animals not only enriches your community and benefits a cause that’s important to you, but it also makes you feel happier and more grateful.

Helping a child or teen with unhealthy social media use
Childhood and the teenage years can be filled with developmental challenges and social pressures. For some kids, social media has a way of exacerbating those problems and fueling anxiety, bullying, depression, and issues with self-esteem. If you’re worried about your child’s social media use, it can be tempting to simply confiscate their phone or other device. But that can create further problems, separating your child from their friends and the positive aspects of social media. Instead, there are other ways to help your child use Facebook, Instagram, and other platforms in a more responsible way.

Monitor and limit your child’s social media use. The more you know about how your child is interacting on social media, the better you’ll be able to address any problems. Parental control apps can help limit your child’s data usage or restrict their phone use to certain times of the day. You can also adjust privacy settings on the different platforms to limit their potential exposure to bullies or predators.

Talk to your child about underlying issues. Problems with social media use can often mask deeper issues. Is your child having problems fitting in at school? Are they suffering from shyness or social anxiety? Are problems at home causing them stress?

Enforce “social media” breaks. For example, you could ban social media until your child has completed their homework in the evening, not allow phones at the dinner table or in their bedroom, and plan family activities that preclude the use of phones or other devices. To prevent sleep problems, always insist phones are turned off at least one hour before bed.

Teach your child how social media is not an accurate reflection of people’s lives. They shouldn’t compare themselves or their lives negatively to others on social media. People only post what they want others to see. Images are manipulated or carefully posed and selected. And having fewer friends on social media doesn’t make your child less popular or less worthy.

Encourage exercise and offline interests. Get your child away from social media by encouraging them to pursue physical activities and hobbies that involve real-world interaction. Exercise is great for relieving anxiety and stress, boosting self-esteem, and improving mood—and is something you can do as a family. The more engaged your child is offline, the less their mood and sense of self-worth will be dependent on how many friends, likes, or shares they have on social media.

Suppose a client sends a POST request to a RESTful HTTP endpoint to create a resource. Which header should the HTTP server include in the response, to indicate the location of the newly created resource?
A. content
B. Location
C. Link
D. Server

The header that the HTTP server should include in the response to indicate the location of the newly created resource is B. Location.

When a client sends a POST request to create a new resource, the server should respond with a 201 Created status code, indicating that the request was successful and a new resource has been created. The response should include a Location header that specifies the URI of the newly created resource. This header is essential for the client to know where to find the new resource.

Option A (content) is a generic header that refers to the content of the response message, but it does not specify the location of the newly created resource.

Option C (Link) is used to specify links to related resources, but it does not provide the URI of the newly created resource.

Option D (Server) is a header that provides information about the server software, but it does not indicate the location of the newly created resource.

What is one difference between the POST and GET HTTP method requests?
A. There's a limit to how many times per second POST requests can be cached by a browser.
B. Unlike GET requests, POST method request are always idempotent.
C. Post requests remain in the browser history only until the window is closed.
D. There's a length limit of the data you send with GET requests.

The one difference between the POST and GET HTTP method requests is D. There's a length limit of the data you send with GET requests.

The GET method is used to request a resource from the server, and the requested resource is included in the URL. GET requests have a length limit on the amount of data that can be sent with the request, typically around 2048 characters. This limit is imposed by most web browsers and servers and is intended to prevent abuse of the GET method.

The POST method is used to submit data to the server, typically to create or update a resource on the server. POST requests do not have a length limit on the amount of data that can be sent with the request. However, the server may impose its own limits on the size of the request payload.

Option A is incorrect because there is no limit to how many times per second POST requests can be cached by a browser.

Option B is incorrect because unlike GET requests, POST method requests are not always idempotent. POST requests may have side effects on the server, such as creating a new resource, and sending the same request multiple times may result in different outcomes.

Option C is incorrect because both POST and GET requests can remain in the browser history until the window is closed.

Say you work for a startup that has hit hockey stick growth. You need to horizontally scale the database to support the increased usage. What are the trade offs between replicating vs sharding your database?
A. Replication maintains better consistency guarantees at the cost of partitioning tolerance.
B. Sharding makes partition tolerance hard but support better availability 
C. Replication is easy, but does not bring all the benefit of complete shardin, if you can fully shared, you should.
D. Sharding enable increased storage capacity and higher load.

The trade-offs between replicating vs sharding your database for horizontal scaling in a high-growth startup are:

A. Replication maintains better consistency guarantees at the cost of partitioning tolerance.
Replication involves creating multiple copies of the same database across different servers, allowing for improved availability and read scalability. However, it can be challenging to maintain consistency across multiple copies, particularly in write-intensive applications. In addition, replication does not provide a way to scale writes beyond the capacity of a single node, which limits its effectiveness in high-write environments.

B. Sharding makes partition tolerance hard but supports better availability.
Sharding involves dividing a large database into smaller, more manageable parts called shards, each stored on a separate server. This allows for improved write scalability and increased storage capacity. However, sharding can be complex to implement, particularly when dealing with data relationships that span multiple shards. In addition, shard failures can cause partitioning problems, which can be challenging to resolve.

C. Replication is easy, but does not bring all the benefits of complete sharding. If you can fully shard, you should.
Replication is relatively easy to implement and can provide immediate performance improvements with minimal changes to the existing architecture. However, replication does not provide the same level of scalability and partition tolerance as complete sharding, and it may not be sufficient for long-term growth.

D. Sharding enables increased storage capacity and higher load.
Sharding allows for increased storage capacity and the ability to handle higher loads by distributing data across multiple servers. This can provide improved scalability and availability for high-growth startups, enabling them to handle increasing amounts of data and traffic.

Ultimately, the choice between replication and sharding depends on the specific needs of the application, including the level of read/write intensity, the data relationships involved, and the growth projections. In some cases, a combination of both replication and sharding may be the most effective solution.

Which of the following Git commands can help you trace a bug in a large commit history?
A. git log
B. git bisect
C. git reflog
D. git history

The Git command that can help you trace a bug in a large commit history is B. git bisect.

Git bisect is a command that can help you find the commit that introduced a bug. It works by performing a binary search on the commit history, starting from a known good commit and a known bad commit. It automatically checks out a series of intermediate commits, asking you to test each one until it finds the commit that introduced the bug.

Option A (git log) is a command that displays the commit history, but it does not provide a systematic way to search for a specific commit or bug.

Option C (git reflog) is a command that shows the history of branch references and can be used to recover lost commits, but it does not help with tracing a bug in a large commit history.

Option D (git history) is not a valid Git command.

Why are RESTful APIs considered stateless?
A. Because they are designed for stateless applications
B. REST architecture prescribes stateless design, but RESTful APIs are actually stateful
C. Because they don't change the state of application; they only expose it to other applications
D. Because the don't track application state; each API request is self-contained and handled in isolation

The reason why RESTful APIs are considered stateless is because of option D: They don't track application state; each API request is self-contained and handled in isolation.

In RESTful architecture, each API request is independent and self-contained, containing all the information required to complete the request. The server does not maintain any information or state about the client between requests. This means that each request can be processed independently of any previous or subsequent requests.

By being stateless, RESTful APIs provide several advantages, including scalability, simplicity, and reliability. Stateless APIs are easier to cache and distribute across multiple servers, as they do not require the servers to share any state. They also reduce the complexity of the server-side implementation and improve fault tolerance by reducing the amount of state that can be lost in the event of a failure.

Option A is incorrect because RESTful APIs can be used in both stateful and stateless applications.

Option B is incorrect because RESTful APIs that are designed according to the REST architecture are intended to be stateless, and stateful APIs would not conform to the architectural principles of REST.

Option C is incorrect because RESTful APIs can change the state of the application, but they do not maintain any information about the client's state between requests.

Which of the following is the least effective way of ensuring loose coupling in software systems?
A. Publish-Subscribe Pattern
B. Dependency Injection
C. Inheritance
D. Observer Pattern

The least effective way of ensuring loose coupling in software systems is C. Inheritance.

Inheritance is a programming concept that allows one class to inherit properties and methods from another class. While inheritance can be useful for code reuse, it can also lead to tight coupling between classes. This is because changes to the superclass can have unintended effects on the subclass, and vice versa, making it difficult to modify the system without affecting other parts of the code.

On the other hand, options A (Publish-Subscribe Pattern), B (Dependency Injection), and D (Observer Pattern) are all effective ways of ensuring loose coupling in software systems. These patterns promote decoupling between components by defining clear interfaces and separating concerns.

The Publish-Subscribe Pattern allows components to communicate with each other without requiring direct references between them. This reduces coupling and promotes flexibility in the system.

Dependency Injection is a technique that separates the creation of an object from its dependencies, allowing components to be easily replaced or modified without affecting other parts of the system.

The Observer Pattern promotes loose coupling by allowing components to subscribe to events and receive updates without requiring direct references between them. This allows components to be easily modified or replaced without affecting the rest of the system.

Hi, I got a job in a code teaching company as Communication Training Expert. Can you help me to design a curriculum for teaching the below listed problems?

Based on the information you provided, it sounds like you are looking to create a course to help engineering students improve their English language skills, with a focus on writing. Is that correct?

If so, here are some suggestions for how you could structure your curriculum:

A kidney stone detection system is a great machine learning project idea, especially since kidney stones are a common and painful medical condition that affects millions of people worldwide. Here are some steps you could take to build a kidney stone detection system using machine learning:

1. Collect Data: The first step in building any machine learning model is to collect data. In the case of a kidney stone detection system, you would need to collect medical images of the kidneys and urinary tract, along with information about the patients' age, gender, and medical history.
2. Label Data: Once you have collected the data, you will need to label it. In the case of a kidney stone detection system, you would need to label the images based on whether or not they show evidence of a kidney stone.
3. Preprocess Data: After labeling the data, you will need to preprocess it. This could involve resizing the images, normalizing the pixel values, and augmenting the data to increase the size of your training set.
4. Build a Model: Next, you will need to build a machine learning model that can classify the images as either showing evidence of a kidney stone or not. You could use a convolutional neural network (CNN) for this task, which is a type of deep learning algorithm that is well-suited to image classification tasks.
5. Train the Model: Once you have built the model, you will need to train it on your labeled and preprocessed data. This involves feeding the data into the model and adjusting the model's weights to minimize the loss function.
6. Evaluate the Model: After training the model, you will need to evaluate its performance on a validation set. This will help you determine how well the model is able to classify new images that it has not seen before.
7. Test the Model: Finally, you will need to test the model on a separate test set to see how well it performs in real-world scenarios. If the model performs well, it could be used to help doctors diagnose kidney stones more quickly and accurately.

A car prices prediction system is a great machine learning project idea. The goal of this project would be to build a machine learning model that can predict the prices of cars based on factors like the car's age, make, model, mileage, and other features. Here are some steps you could take to build a car prices prediction system using machine learning:

1. Collect Data: The first step in building any machine learning model is to collect data. In the case of a car prices prediction system, you would need to collect data on the prices of cars, along with information about the cars' age, make, model, mileage, and other features. You could collect this data from online marketplaces or from car dealerships.
2. Preprocess Data: Once you have collected the data, you will need to preprocess it. This could involve removing any missing or duplicate data, encoding categorical variables, and scaling numerical features.
3. Feature Engineering: After preprocessing the data, you will need to engineer new features that can help the machine learning model make more accurate predictions. For example, you could create a new feature that calculates the average mileage per year for each car.
4. Split Data: Next, you will need to split the data into training, validation, and test sets. The training set will be used to train the machine learning model, while the validation set will be used to tune the model's hyperparameters. The test set will be used to evaluate the model's performance on new data.
5. Build a Model: Once you have preprocessed the data and engineered new features, you will need to build a machine learning model that can predict car prices. You could use a regression algorithm, such as linear regression or a decision tree regressor, for this task.
6. Train the Model: After building the model, you will need to train it on the training set. This involves feeding the data into the model and adjusting the model's weights to minimize the loss function.
7. Tune Hyperparameters: Once you have trained the model, you will need to tune its hyperparameters using the validation set. This involves adjusting the model's settings, such as the learning rate or the number of hidden layers in a neural network, to improve its performance.
8. Evaluate the Model: After tuning the hyperparameters, you will need to evaluate the model's performance on the test set. This will help you determine how well the model is able to predict car prices on new data.
9. Deploy the Model: Finally, if the model performs well, you could deploy it to a web application or a mobile app that allows users to enter information about a car and get an estimated price. This could be useful for car dealerships or for anyone who is looking to buy or sell a car.

MERCURY KX ANNOUNCES JEAN-MICHEL BLAIS' NEW EP "SERENADES"
Montreal-based post-classical pianist and composer Jean-Michel Blais is pleased to announce his new EP “Serenades”, out March 10th on Mercury KX. The EP is the solo piano companion to Blais’ highly successful and loved album “Aubades”, released this past February.
“Serenades” is the nocturnal companion to the brightness of “Aubades”, and features intimate and atmospheric solo piano versions of key tracks from the album, including “Amour”, “Ouessant”, and “Murmures”. It also features three new tracks: “117 (Bach)”, “La Chute”, and “Morning (Improv)”. “La Chute” (“The Fall”) was especially composed for the left hand, due to a right arm injury Blais suffered after falling on ice before a performance. This inspired him to write this piece particularly for the left hand. The first single “Ouessant” (Piano) is a melancholic slow wander through luminous and soothing solo piano soundscapes.
“Serenades” is a testament to Blais’ musical prowess and ability to create captivating and moving musical landscapes. It’s sure to be a stunning addition to Blais’ already impressive discography.

JEAN-MICHEL BLAIS DECCA BIO:
Jean-Michel Blais - Biography
 
Jean-Michel Blais is a post-classical pianist and composer living in Montreal, Canada.
 
Blais grew up in a rural French Catholic town in Quebec and began tinkering on his family's organ at age 9. By 11, he began writing original compositions and taking piano lessons. A natural talent, by 17 he was invited to the Trois-Rivieres Music Conservatory and began training as a classical pianist. The constraints of formal training began to wear on Blais who started to gravitate towards experimentation and improvisation. He eventually left the school and put aside his musical path.
 
In his mid-20s, Blais traveled to Europe and Latin America, studied special education, liberal arts and psychology, while moving to Berlin for about a year and six months in Buenos Aires, before settling in Montreal where he rediscovered his love for composing and performing, which caught the ear of Toronto-based indie label Arts & Crafts.
 
Blais’ debut album, Il, was written and recorded in his apartment over two years of daily improvisations. It was released on April 8, 2016, and amassed over 50 million streams worldwide, reaching #1 on Billboard’s Classical chart in Canada 14 times, earning a Polaris Music Prize long list nomination and a spot on Time Magazine’s Top ten albums of 2016.
 
The following year, Blais performed at the prestigious Red Bull Music Academy in collaboration with Grammy-nominated producer CFCF. This spurred a partnership between the two musicians that yielded Cascades, a collaborative EP released on March 15, 2017. The release earned praises for its brave interplay between piano and electronics, including by the likes of Pitchfork and BBC Radio 6.
 
After a year of touring North America and Europe, Blais began working on his sophomore album, Dans ma main. Released on May 11, 2018. Jean-Michel Blais’ Dans ma main is an expansive album of post-classical piano music with strands of electronic detail. Recorded at night in a Steinway & Sons piano shop, Blais blends his classical skill and precise pop sensibility with synthetic accompaniment, which creates unique environments where the piano’s intimacy can be subtly disrupted at any time. Dans ma main earned a Polaris Music Prize short list nomination.
 
Fascinated with the cathartic response of worldwide audiences to his pin-drop performances, Blais considers how music has served as a tool of wellness in his own life: from his early career in special education, to his parents’ use of ambient music to soothe the Tourettic symptoms he’s been suffering from since childhood. On Dans ma main, Jean-Michel calls upon these periods of his life, reimagining a serenity that he previously found only seated at a piano.
 
In the spring of 2018, when asked by his landlord to leave his beloved apartment where he recorded his debut album Il, Blais invited friends and family in his bedroom to take part into an intimate series of home concert and recording sessions, which gave birth to his sophomore EP, Eviction sessions, released on Novembre 18, 2018.

JEAN-MICHEL BLAIS PREVIOUS ALBUM “aubades”:
aubades, the new album from post-classical piano icon Jean-Michel Blais, marks the Montreal-born musician’s transition from pianist to composer, as he writes for an ensemble for the first time in his career. Written during the pandemic and following a breakup, Blais has used his distinctive musical voice to create a defiantly uplifting record with glistening instrumental textures and warm major tonalities. The album’s title refers to the “aubade”, a Middle Ages morning love song about lovers separating at daybreak, a dawn serenade.
 
Despite the difficult global and personal backdrop, Blais described the time writing this album as a “fruitful moment of creativity for me. We started having hares in the park, beautiful butterflies flying everywhere. It was a time of lots of blossoming, and also a moment when I blossomed from being a pianist into a composer.” Musical ideas captured in over 500 recorded piano improvisations were transformed by Blais into 11 compositions performed by a 12-person ensemble. During the composition process, Blais collaborated with Alex Weston, former music assistant to Philip Glass. The musicians were recorded with close-up microphones, creating a richly intimate atmosphere that captures the human behind each instrument, from the mechanics of the woodwind keys to the snap of a double bass string.

BACKGROUND ARTICLE (ON CLASSIC FM SITE) ON THE HISTORY OF PIECES FOR LEFT HAND PIANO
 
The best classical music for piano left hand
12 August 2014, 12:56 | Updated: 6 January 2017, 14:45
 
Facebook share Twitter share
In celebration of International Left Handers Day, we're investigating the top ten pieces of piano music composed specifically for the left hand, as chosen by one-handed piano virtuoso, Nicholas McCarthy.
 
1. Prelude and Nocturne Op. 9 - Scriabin
 
After injuring his right hand learning some of Liszt's more challenging music, Scriabin was forced to re-evaluate his priorities and change the way he composed, shifting the emphasis onto his uninjured left hand. Much of the music he went on to write sounds fiendishly difficult, making the most of the capabilities of his only working hand - but he also composed two pieces for left hand only. After much soul-searching, the result is this, one of Scriabin's most beautiful and introspective pieces.
 
 
 
 
 
2. Piano Concerto for the Left Hand - Ravel
 
Even before this piece was premiered in 1932, pianist Alfred Cortot had made an arrangement for piano two-hands and orchestra. But Ravel, who wrote the music especially for Austrian pianist Paul Wittgenstein after he lost his arm in the war, forbade the performance of the new arrangement. It's not a wonder he was frustrated: before writing the work, he studied Saint-Saëns' left hand études religiously, absorbing as many of his techniques as possible.
 
 
 
 
3. Chaconne in D minor - Bach/Brahms
 
ADVERTISING
 
There's nothing like getting stuck in to some truly meaty Bach keyboard music. So, imagine Clara Schumann's frustration when she injured her right hand and could no longer play the music she knew and loved. Luckily, Brahms came to the rescue with this ingenious arrangement of his hypnotic Chaconne in D minor. It may only be played with one hand, but Brahms manages to bring out every one of Bach's original voices. Genius.
 
 
 
 
4. Etude Op. 25 No. 12 'Ocean' - Chopin arr. Godowsky
 
They're known as the most difficult piano pieces in the left-hand repertoire, and after listening to these waves upon waves of notes, it's easy to see why. Chopin's études - challenging enough at the best of times - have been transformed by Polish composer Godowsky into a series of incredible studies and mind-bending technical exercises, including transferring the technically difficult passages between hands, interweaving separate études to form one individual piece, and transcribing this entire étude for left hand solo.
 
 
 
5. Three Improvisations - Bridge
 
Eerie and beautiful, these 'improvisations' by English composer Frank Bridge were written for his friend Douglas Fox, who lost his arm during the First World War. With sparing use of the piano pedals, the heavy bass chords ring out as the left hand glides over the piano's upper register.
 
 
 
 
6. Piano Concerto for Left Hand - Korngold
 
The man behind Ravel's piano concerto for left hand, Paul Wittgenstein, is really the man to thank when it comes to commissioning music for the left hand. He approached Richard Strauss, Prokofiev, and Hindemith - but it's Korngold who stole the show. Wittgenstein was so happy with this work that he commissioned another piece, a suite for 2 violins, cello and piano left-hand, Op. 23.
 
 
 
 
7. Diversions - Britten
 
Looking at the score, it's hard to believe this explosive piece is written for just one hand. With its cascading quavers and impressive passages jumping across the entire length of the piano, this is the ultimate in left hand piano fireworks - and once again, we have Paul Wittgenstein to thank for its existence.
 
 
 
 
8. Piano Sonata in C Minor - Reinecke
 
While Reinecke may not be the most high-profile of composers, his Opus 179 sonata has made its mark on the world of left hand piano music. There's a reason for that: give this a listen for a healthy dose of bombastic, Romantic piano music.
 
 
 
9. Six études for the left hand - Saint-Saëns
 
Despite their sheer brilliance, Saint-Saëns' piano études are often overlooked. Every movement requires absolute independence for each individual finger, regardless of hand shape or strength. It's these pieces Ravel studied so diligently when embarking on his left hand piano concerto, and it's easy to see why.
 
 
 
10. 4 Special Studies - Reger
 
German composer Max Reger brings grace and personality to these cheery piano works, making even the most simple techniques sound impressive. Watch how the left hand trips over the piano with ease, bringing out individual parts across the entire range of the piano.
