CHAPTER FIFTEEN
 RULE #6
 Crawl Before You Walk, Walk Before You Run
 From Jeremy
As you know by now, the essence of the protocol is lifelong behavioral change. That means changing the way you carry yourself and move (the neutral spine and all that). But it also means adopting and staying with our carefully designed (and progressive) exercise regimen. It is designed for back pain and it is essential for a permanent cure. And you have to do it correctly. There is an awful lot of text and a bunch of pictures about exercise in this book. But it’s not because you have to do so many exercises; there really aren’t that many. It’s because we want you to do them right. Remember, you are far more likely to do these exercises with the compensatory patterns you have been doing for years unless you read closely and concentrate on doing them the right way. Doing them the wrong way will just make you better at doing bad things. 
One great key to doing them right is to go slow in the learning process. Whether you’re the type or not, you have to take it step-by-step. This is a healing process, and you cannot rush a healing process; it has its own rhythm and you have to follow it. Roll out these exercises slowly over time, progressing from one level to the next only when you “get” the level you are on and are ready to move up. 
Remember, it is these daily exercises that—more than anything else—are going to give most of you a significant and permanent reduction in pain and tightness. Other steps get you past the immediate pain. These steps make the change permanent. 
Be Still to Heal; Be Still to Stay Well 
One of the main goals of changing your movement patterns is to make the “be still to heal” concept (Rule #2, in Chapter 7) part of your daily life. Once you have achieved an initial fix, you don’t have to be as still. But it is a great idea to learn to move with relative stillness (without moving your lumbar spine too much) all the time. 
This is a big change for most of you, and you have to take it in steps; as I say, you have to crawl before you walk. The first step in learning to keep your lumbar spine still routinely is simply to learn to march in place, with a neutral spine. (This is the “crawl” part of the process; it may seem too easy to bother with. It is not.) The goal is to learn to march in place with no lower spine movement. Then you can move on to other movements in this chapter.
Key tip: If any of these movements hurt, take a step back to the last thing you could do without pain (marching in place, for instance). Stay with that for a few more days and then try to progress again. In theory, if you can march in place without pain, you can walk without pain and move without pain. It’s just a matter of building up your core, turning on the right muscles, and learning to use those muscles to move in the right way. Whenever you have difficulty, go back to the previous step and start again. 
Okay, you’ve done marching in place without moving your lower spine. Once that feels relatively simple, try doing this while walking. Things become a little murkier here because there are many factors at play. Because you are now weight-bearing, you need the support of important muscle groups like the glutes and latissimus dorsi (lats) to support your spine, in addition to the muscles we talked about in the core. If you can’t do this at first without pain, follow the advice in the book for a while, and it will come once you have developed enough strength to support your spine while standing. 
Walking with Neutral Spine 
Learning to walk with a neutral spine means applying the same mechanics of your Slow March with Neutral Spine exercise to an upright, weight-bearing position. Almost everyone can improve their ability to walk without pain if they can do the slow march exercise on the ground. The distance you can walk without pain, or without significant increases in pain, will increase as you build core and gluteal strength and endurance by doing the exercises in this book. To get started on increasing your ability to walk with decreased pain, follow these directions:
Step 1: Stand with good posture. Stand tall, shoulders back, head over your shoulders. Think of sticking your chest out and pulling your shoulder blades down and back. Make sure you aren’t leaning forward. Tilt your pelvis back and forth (just like you did on the floor earlier), and stop where your lower back feels the most comfortable. 
Step 2: Use your core to lock your neutral spine in place. 
Step 3: Now try to walk while maintaining the position of your lower back and with the good posture you just set. It can help to put one hand on your stomach and one on your low back to feel for movement. Once you feel like you’ve mastered maintaining neutral spine while walking, start to swing your arms from the shoulders, arm to opposite leg. This means if your right leg is swinging forward, your left arm should be as well. Make sure to always swing your arms from the shoulders as you walk; this helps dissipate force away from the spine. This movement takes time to master so don’t get frustrated. Keep practicing. It gets easier after you have built endurance and strength in the muscles that support the spine. 
Step 4: If you sense your back is about to hurt, stop and have a seat for a few minutes and give your back a break. Then try walking a little longer. Over time, the period between these sitting breaks should increase, allowing you to walk farther without pain.
Now let’s move on to more complex movements. To make it easier (and to improve your chances of success) we are going to break those more complex movements down into pieces and then put them all together at the end. We will start with movement from the hips, a very basic move in all our lives all the time. Let’s start with hip-hinging. Hip-hinging is a way to bend forward without stressing (i.e., bending) the spine. Done right, the axis of movement is in the hips, not the low back. Your low back should be in a protected neutral position with the core engaged. As you bend forward, your low back doesn’t round or move. Let’s look at a picture of hip-hinging and a picture of lumbar flexion (low back bending); one is good, the other is bad. This is simple stuff, but it is very hard to get over your ancient, bad habits. Hard but essential. 

Hip-Hinge with Neutral Spine
Here are a couple of pictures showing you the right way to hinge (on the left) and the wrong way (on the right). Notice the very slightly curved lower back in the hip-hinge picture on the left: no movement. Excellent! The pic on the right has quite a lot of spine movement; don’t do that. When you first try this movement, it may feel awkward, and you may assume that you cannot reach as far forward with good posture. Not so. You’ll soon realize that you can move just as far forward, using only your hips, as you did before, using your back. You don’t lose anything and you avoid a ton of pain. Over time you will gain mobility. The reason is that moving with your lower back eventually causes pain and stiffness in your back. But moving from your hips will not have that result. Let’s practice doing it right. 
Right Wrong

Step 1: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart.
Step 2: Set your posture: Find neutral spine, brace the core, shoulders back, stand as tall as you can without pain and without breaking neutral spine. 
Step 3: Bend your knees slightly. 
Step 4: Feel the two bones sticking out of the sides of your hips. These are called your greater trochanters. Picture a pole going through these two points, passing right through your pelvis. This is your imaginary axis of movement.
Step 5: Now place one hand on your stomach and the other on your back. On your back, put your fingers on those little bones sticking out (spinous processes). You are putting your hands here to feel for any unwanted movement when you hip-hinge. 
Step 6: Without moving anything in the core (you shouldn’t feel those little bones in your back moving), slowly bend forward on that imaginary axis of movement through the hip bones while sticking your buttocks out behind you a little bit. If you feel your stomach or back move, stop, reset, and try again.

Step 7: Go only as far forward as you can without moving your back. For many of you, your hamstrings (back of legs) are going to be a limiting factor in your range of motion in the beginning. Don’t worry if you can’t go too far. Your hamstrings will loosen up in time if you follow all of the advice in this book. 
Step 8: Slowly return to the starting position without moving your back or stomach. 
Step 9: This should not hurt your low back. If it does, reset your stance, find neutral spine, brace your core. Not hurting? Now try it again with a very small range of motion. Stay within the range of motion that doesn’t hurt. Slowly increase your range of motion over time—it will come. 
Adding Rotation
Now let’s get a little more complex. Let’s add rotation to the bend. Hip-hinging correctly allows you to move forward and backward without pain. Now let’s learn to move side to side. We are talking rotation. Again, think about moving from the hips. It’s easiest to visualize proper rotation if you think about your waist. We don’t want any movement at the waist. Try to picture your rib cage being locked on to your pelvis. That’s what you want. Pretend that there are no joints between your ribs and pelvis, for this one. 
Full-Body Rotation
The chest, navel, and hips all move together. This means there is no (or very little) movement in the spine. The body rotates by moving the hips, knees, and pelvis. If the hips and pelvis stay facing forward while the rib cage moves, the movement comes from the waist, and therefore the lumbar spine. This type of movement can wear down the discs and joints in the spine over time, especially when additional load is applied (like unloading the dishwasher for instance). Just like hip-hinging, rotation done properly doesn’t cause you to lose mobility and will improve athletic performance. Let’s give it a try. Once again, the new movement is not that hard. But you are trying to unlearn a lifetime of moving with your spine. That is hard. 

Step 1: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Step 2: Set your posture: Find neutral spine, brace the core, shoulders back, stand as tall as you can without pain and without breaking neutral spine. 
Step 3: Bend your knees slightly. 
Step 4: Place your hands on the top of your pelvis with your fingers pointed forward. This hand position is to help you visualize movement of the pelvis as you get used to this movement. 
Step 5: Keep the knees bent and loose but the core engaged, and try to turn a little to the right by shifting the knees and hips. Your fingers, navel, hip bones, chest bone, and face should all point the same direction. It’s okay if you pivot the feet a little bit. 

Step 6: Now try to the left.
Step 7: Slowly increase your range of motion by bending more at the knees. 
Step 8: Then increase your range of motion even more by pivoting your feet. 
Step 9: At all times and especially at the end of the movement, your chest bone, head, navel, and hips should be in alignment. 
TROUBLESHOOTING
Knee pain: Stay lighter on your feet\ and pivot the ball of the inside foot (the side you are moving away from) as you rotate. 
Torso Rotation with Hip-Hinge and Squat
Now let’s try to put those two movements—the hip-hinge and the rotation—together with a squat for a realistic, three-dimensional movement that allows you to accomplish everyday tasks (think unloading the dishwasher, putting up groceries) without stressing your back. You are going to rotate and bend down (hip-hinge), then come back up to the other side and extend. 
Notice that within that entire chain of movement, the person in the drawing does not really move his spine (look at the waist if you’re having trouble visualizing that). The rib cage remains locked onto the pelvis the entire time. These pictures show you that you can go almost 180 degrees from side to side, stooping as low as you can and then extending as high as your hands can go, without compromising your back. Let’s try it.
Step 1: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Step 2: Find neutral spine and engage your core to lock it in. 
Step 3: Stand with good posture. 
Step 4: Rotate torso to the right without twisting the waist as before.

Revenue Operations in Marketing: The Key to Driving Growth and Optimizing Performance

Revenue operations (RevOps) is a relatively new term that has gained a lot of attention in the marketing industry in recent years. It refers to the integration of marketing, sales, and customer success teams to align efforts and maximize revenue growth. The goal of RevOps is to streamline processes, eliminate silos, and create a unified approach to driving growth and optimizing performance.

Traditionally, marketing, sales, and customer success teams have operated as separate units with distinct goals and objectives. However, this siloed approach can lead to inefficiencies, misalignment, and missed opportunities. By integrating these teams into a single RevOps function, organizations can create a more cohesive, data-driven approach to driving revenue growth.

The benefits of revenue operations in marketing are numerous. By aligning efforts and streamlining processes, organizations can:

* Improve lead quality and conversion rates
* Increase sales velocity and close deals faster
* Enhance customer satisfaction and reduce churn
* Increase the overall efficiency of marketing, sales, and customer success teams
* Improve data-driven decision making
* Enhance the overall customer experience

To get started with revenue operations in marketing, organizations need to take a few key steps:

1. Define your revenue operations objectives: Start by defining what you want to achieve with your RevOps efforts. This may include increasing lead quality, improving sales velocity, or reducing customer churn.
2. Align your teams: Ensure that your marketing, sales, and customer success teams are all aligned and working together towards a common goal. This may involve regular cross-functional meetings, collaboration on data analysis, and clear communication channels.
3. Optimize your processes: Streamline your processes and eliminate inefficiencies by using technology and data to automate manual tasks and drive better decision making.
4. Measure your success: Regularly measure the success of your RevOps efforts and make data-driven decisions to continuously improve performance.
5. Invest in technology: Invest in technology that can help you streamline processes, align teams, and drive better decision making. Some of the key tools used in revenue operations include marketing automation platforms, customer relationship management (CRM) software, and data analytics tools.

One of the key benefits of revenue operations in marketing is the ability to make data-driven decisions. By integrating data from multiple sources and teams, organizations can gain a more complete understanding of their customers, prospects, and revenue performance. This data can be used to inform strategy, optimize processes, and make better decisions that drive growth and maximize performance.

Another important aspect of revenue operations in marketing is customer experience. By aligning efforts across marketing, sales, and customer success teams, organizations can create a more cohesive and seamless experience for their customers. This can lead to higher customer satisfaction, reduced churn, and ultimately, higher revenue growth.

In conclusion, revenue operations in marketing is a critical component of any organization's growth strategy. By aligning efforts and streamlining processes, organizations can drive better performance, increase revenue growth, and enhance the customer experience. To get started with RevOps, organizations should define their objectives, align teams, optimize processes, measure success, and invest in technology. With the right approach, revenue operations can help organizations take their marketing efforts to the next level and achieve long-term success.
Write me a blog on new approaches to marketing lead distribution

New Approaches to Marketing Lead Distribution: Maximizing Efficiency and Driving Results

Lead distribution is a critical component of any marketing strategy. It refers to the process of distributing leads from marketing campaigns to the sales team for follow-up and conversion. Effective lead distribution can help organizations maximize the return on their marketing investment and drive better results.

Traditionally, lead distribution was a manual process, with leads being distributed manually to sales representatives or assigned to specific territories. However, with the rise of technology and data analytics, there are now new and more efficient approaches to lead distribution that can help organizations maximize efficiency and drive better results.

1. Predictive Lead Scoring

Predictive lead scoring is a data-driven approach to lead distribution that uses machine learning algorithms to determine the likelihood of a lead converting into a customer. Leads are assigned a score based on a variety of factors, such as demographic data, behavior data, and previous interactions with the brand. Sales teams can then prioritize their follow-up efforts based on these scores, allowing them to focus on the most promising leads and increase their chances of conversion.

2. Lead Routing

Lead routing is another new approach to lead distribution that uses technology to automate the process of assigning leads to the most appropriate sales representative. Leads are automatically assigned based on a variety of factors, such as location, product interest, and previous interactions with the brand. This approach helps ensure that leads are quickly and efficiently assigned to the right salesperson, increasing the chances of conversion and improving overall efficiency.

3. Lead Nurturing

Lead nurturing is another key component of modern lead distribution strategies. This involves using marketing automation and other technologies to engage with leads over time and build relationships. Lead nurturing can help increase the chances of conversion by providing relevant information and insights to leads at the right time, keeping them engaged and interested in your products or services.

4. Data-Driven Insights

Data-driven insights are another important aspect of modern lead distribution strategies. By integrating data from multiple sources, such as CRM systems, marketing automation platforms, and other sources, organizations can gain a more complete understanding of their leads and the best approach for reaching them. This data can be used to inform lead distribution strategies and improve efficiency, helping organizations drive better results from their marketing efforts.

In conclusion, lead distribution is a critical component of any marketing strategy. With the rise of technology and data analytics, there are now new and more efficient approaches to lead distribution that can help organizations maximize efficiency and drive better results. These include predictive lead scoring, lead routing, lead nurturing, and data-driven insights. By incorporating these new approaches into their lead distribution strategies, organizations can achieve better results from their marketing efforts and drive more revenue.

Suppose you have a social media application that allows users to create posts, comment on posts, and like posts. You have an AppSync API that handles these operations, and a set of Lambda functions that are triggered by events from the API. For example, when a new post is created, a Lambda function might be triggered to perform some processing on the post, such as analyzing the sentiment of the post or generating a thumbnail image.

Now suppose you want to add a new feature to your application: real-time notifications for users when someone likes or comments on their posts. To implement this feature, you need a way to trigger notifications in real-time based on events that occur in your application.

This is where AWS EventBridge comes in. You can use AWS EventBridge to create a central event bus that can receive and process events from your AppSync API and Lambda functions. When a user likes or comments on a post, your AppSync API can publish an event to the event bus, which triggers a Lambda function to send a notification to the user.

By using AWS EventBridge, you can create a scalable and event-driven architecture that can respond to changes and updates in real-time. You can use the event bus to trigger workflows, run Lambda functions, send notifications, and more, all based on events that occur in your application.

In summary, AWS EventBridge can be a powerful tool for building event-driven architectures that can respond to changes and updates in real-time. By integrating AWS EventBridge with your AppSync API and other services, you can create complex workflows and automate business processes, all while reducing the amount of custom code and complexity required in your architecture.

what's the difference between AWS Kinesis Data Streams and Eventbridge

AWS Kinesis Data Streams and AWS EventBridge are both event-driven services offered by AWS, but they have different use cases and functionality.

Here are some of the key differences between AWS Kinesis Data Streams and AWS EventBridge:

1. Data type: AWS Kinesis Data Streams is designed for streaming data and works best for high-volume data sources, such as IoT devices or clickstreams. It is optimized for handling data in real-time and supports custom data formats. AWS EventBridge, on the other hand, is designed for event-based data and works best for integrating with different services and systems to trigger actions based on specific events.
2. Data processing: AWS Kinesis Data Streams allows you to process and analyze data in real-time using services like AWS Lambda or Kinesis Data Analytics. It also offers features like data retention and automatic data sharding for scaling your data processing. AWS EventBridge is a fully managed event bus that can capture and route events between different services and targets, but it does not provide built-in data processing features like Kinesis Data Streams.
3. Event management: AWS EventBridge provides a centralized event bus that allows you to capture and route events from a variety of sources to different targets, such as AWS Lambda functions or SNS topics. It also offers features like event filtering and pattern matching to selectively route events to different targets. AWS Kinesis Data Streams, on the other hand, is focused on data processing and does not offer the same level of event management and routing capabilities as AWS EventBridge.

In summary, AWS Kinesis Data Streams and AWS EventBridge are both powerful event-driven services offered by AWS, but they have different use cases and functionality. If you need to process high-volume streaming data, AWS Kinesis Data Streams may be the best option. If you need to integrate different services and systems and trigger actions based on specific events, AWS EventBridge may be the best option.

While it's possible to directly invoke one Lambda function from another using the `Invoke` API or by configuring the source Lambda function to trigger the destination Lambda function, using AWS EventBridge has some advantages over direct invocation.

Here are a few reasons why you might want to use AWS EventBridge:

1. Decoupling: By using EventBridge to trigger your Lambda functions, you can decouple your functions from one another and create a more loosely coupled architecture. This can make your architecture more flexible and easier to maintain, as each function can be developed and deployed independently without worrying about breaking dependencies.
2. Scalability: EventBridge is a scalable and highly available event bus that can handle large volumes of events and automatically scale to meet demand. By using EventBridge, you can ensure that your functions are triggered reliably and can handle a high volume of events without being overwhelmed.
3. Visibility: EventBridge provides a centralized location for monitoring and managing events across your architecture. By using EventBridge, you can easily track the flow of events between different services and identify potential issues or bottlenecks in your architecture.
4. Integration: EventBridge integrates with a wide variety of AWS services and can be used to trigger actions in response to events from those services. By using EventBridge, you can create powerful workflows and automations that span multiple services and trigger actions based on specific events.

Overall, while it's possible to directly invoke one Lambda function from another, using AWS EventBridge can provide some benefits, such as decoupling, scalability, visibility, and integration. By using EventBridge to trigger your Lambda functions, you can create a more flexible and scalable architecture that can respond to changes and updates in real-time.
What could go wrong if i Invoke from the lambda

There are a few potential issues to consider when directly invoking one Lambda function from another:

1. Tight coupling: When one Lambda function directly invokes another, it creates a tight coupling between the two functions. This means that changes to one function can potentially affect the behavior of the other function. This can make it harder to make changes to your architecture, as you need to carefully manage the dependencies between your functions.
2. Scalability: Directly invoking one Lambda function from another can potentially limit the scalability of your architecture. When one function is invoked synchronously from another, it can create a bottleneck that limits the overall throughput of your architecture. This can be particularly problematic when you need to handle a high volume of requests or process large amounts of data.
3. Resilience: When one Lambda function directly invokes another, it can create a single point of failure in your architecture. If the destination function is unavailable or experiencing issues, it can prevent the source function from completing its processing. This can make your architecture less resilient and increase the risk of downtime or errors.
4. Monitoring: When one Lambda function directly invokes another, it can be harder to monitor the flow of data and events between your functions. This can make it harder to identify issues or track the behavior of your architecture over time.

Overall, while it's possible to directly invoke one Lambda function from another, doing so can create potential issues with tight coupling, scalability, resilience, and monitoring. By using AWS EventBridge or another event-driven service, you can create a more flexible and scalable architecture that can respond to changes and updates in real-time, while reducing the risks associated with direct invocation.

The return to capital investment is different from the return to investment in assets in the Euler equation in the RBC model because they involve different factors.

The Euler equation for investment in physical capital takes into account the return to investment in the form of the marginal product of capital (R\_t+1 - delta), where R\_t+1 is the interest rate and delta is the depreciation rate of the capital stock. This represents the expected increase in future output that will result from investing in capital today, and it is discounted by the household's discount factor and expected inflation rate.

On the other hand, the Euler equation for investment in financial assets takes into account the return on those assets in the form of the interest rate (R\_t+1), which represents the expected return on financial assets that will be earned in the future. This is also discounted by the household's discount factor and expected inflation rate.

Thus, the return to capital investment is influenced by the marginal product of capital, which is affected by both the interest rate and the depreciation rate of capital, while the return to investment in financial assets is determined purely by the interest rate.

When solving the firm's problem in the RBC model, we maximize profits w.r.t capital and labour and get that wage = MPL and interest rate = MPK. Is this correct? Explain the economic intuition behind this

Yes, that's correct. In the RBC model, firms are assumed to be perfectly competitive and profit-maximizing. The firm's production function depends on the quantities of capital and labor used in production, and the price of output is assumed to be equal to the marginal product of labor (MPL) times the price of output, which equals the marginal product of capital (MPK) times the rental rate of capital.

The profit-maximizing condition implies that firms will choose the quantities of capital and labor such that the marginal product of each input equals its cost. Therefore, in equilibrium, the wage rate will be equal to the marginal product of labor (MPL), and the rental rate of capital will be equal to the marginal product of capital (MPK).

The economic intuition behind this result is that in a perfectly competitive market, factors of production (labor and capital) are paid their marginal products. This ensures that resources are allocated efficiently, and firms are incentivized to use inputs until their marginal product equals their cost. The equality between the interest rate and the marginal product of capital and between the wage rate and the marginal product of labor represents the optimal allocation of resources in the economy, given the production technology and the preferences of households.

In the context of databases, storage, processing, and analytics, streaming data and batch data are handled differently because they have different characteristics and requirements.

Batch data is typically stored in a database or a data warehouse, where it is organized in tables and indexed for efficient querying. Batch data is processed in large batches or sets of data, typically at scheduled intervals or when a certain amount of data has accumulated. Batch processing is well-suited for applications that require processing large amounts of historical data, such as business intelligence, reporting, and data warehousing.

Streaming data, on the other hand, is generated continuously and is processed in real-time as it arrives. Streaming data is often stored in a distributed storage system such as Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS) or Amazon S3. The processing of streaming data is typically done using stream processing frameworks such as Apache Kafka, Apache Flink, or Amazon Kinesis. Stream processing is well-suited for applications that require real-time analytics, such as fraud detection, predictive maintenance, and monitoring.

In summary, batch processing is suitable for applications that require processing large amounts of historical data, while stream processing is suitable for applications that require real-time analytics on continuously generated data. The choice of storage, processing, and analytics technologies depends on the specific requirements of the application, including performance, scalability, and cost.
Can you explain data indexing in detail, why is it important and show me examples where this done well

Sure, I'd be happy to explain data indexing!

Data indexing is the process of organizing and optimizing data in a database to improve query performance. When a database receives a query, it uses an index to quickly locate the data needed to satisfy the query. Without indexing, the database would have to scan every record in the database to find the requested data, which would be slow and inefficient.

There are several types of data indexing techniques, each with its strengths and weaknesses. Some of the most common types of indexing include:

1. B-Tree Indexing: B-Tree indexing is the most commonly used indexing technique. It organizes data in a tree-like structure, allowing for fast searches on data that is ordered or clustered.
2. Bitmap Indexing: Bitmap indexing is a specialized indexing technique used for columns with a small number of distinct values, such as gender or status. It maps each value to a bitmap, which can be quickly searched to find the desired rows.
3. Hash Indexing: Hash indexing is useful for exact-match searches, where the query matches an entire field value. It creates a hash table that maps the value to a specific location in the database.
4. Full-Text Indexing: Full-text indexing is used to index large blocks of text, such as articles, emails, and other documents. It searches for specific words and phrases within the text, allowing for fast text-based searches.
5. Spatial Indexing: Spatial indexing is used for geographic data, such as maps or GPS coordinates. It organizes data based on location, allowing for fast searches within a specific area.

Indexing is important because it can significantly improve the performance of database queries, especially on large databases. A well-designed index can make the difference between a query that takes seconds to run and one that takes minutes or even hours.

Here is an example of a well-designed index in SQL:
```java
CREATE INDEX idx_customer_name ON customers (last_name, first_name);
```
This creates an index on the `last_name` and `first_name` columns of the `customers` table. When a query is run that searches for a customer by name, the database can use this index to quickly locate the desired record.

Overall, data indexing is an important aspect of database design and optimization, and understanding the various types of indexing techniques can help you design better databases and improve query performance.
How does Amazon do it, can you provide the schema and example codes where possible

Amazon offers various indexing options for its database services, such as Amazon RDS, Amazon DynamoDB, and Amazon Redshift. Here are some examples of how Amazon implements indexing:

1. Amazon RDS:

Amazon RDS offers indexing through the creation of indexes on tables. Users can create primary keys and secondary indexes, which allows for faster querying and filtering of data.

For example, here's how you can create an index in Amazon RDS for a MySQL database:
```java
CREATE INDEX index_name ON table_name (column1, column2);
```
1. Amazon DynamoDB:

Amazon DynamoDB offers automatic indexing through its Global Secondary Index (GSI) feature. Users can define one or more GSIs for their table, which allows for fast and efficient querying of data.

Here's an example of creating a GSI in Amazon DynamoDB:
```css
aws dynamodb update-table --table-name MyTable --attribute-definitions AttributeName=GSIPartitionKey,AttributeType=S AttributeName=GSISortKey,AttributeType=S --global-secondary-index-updates '[{"Create":{"IndexName":"MyGSI","KeySchema":[{"AttributeName":"GSIPartitionKey","KeyType":"HASH"},{"AttributeName":"GSISortKey","KeyType":"RANGE"}],"Projection":{"ProjectionType":"ALL"},"ProvisionedThroughput":{"ReadCapacityUnits":5,"WriteCapacityUnits":5}}}]}'
```
1. Amazon Redshift:

Amazon Redshift offers columnar indexing through its Automatic Vacuum Manager (AVM) feature. The AVM automatically reclaims and compacts space, and it also sorts and organizes data in a way that improves query performance.

Here's an example of creating a sort key in Amazon Redshift:
```sql
CREATE TABLE my_table (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(50),
    age INTEGER
)
DISTSTYLE KEY DISTKEY (id)
SORTKEY (name);
```
Overall, indexing is an important feature in databases as it allows for faster querying and filtering of data, and Amazon offers various indexing options to suit different use cases.
Elaborate on elastic search and how can I use it in my pipeline, give me e2e picture

Elasticsearch is a distributed, open-source search and analytics engine designed for horizontal scalability, high performance, and near real-time search and data analytics. It is commonly used in data pipelines for search, log analysis, and text mining applications.

Here is an end-to-end pipeline example of how Elasticsearch can be used in a data pipeline:

1. Data Ingestion: The first step in the pipeline is to ingest data into Elasticsearch. This can be done through a variety of ways such as APIs, data connectors, log files, or message queues.
2. Data Indexing: Once the data is ingested, the next step is to index it. Elasticsearch creates an inverted index for the data, which enables fast searching and retrieval of data. The index can be customized according to the data structure and search requirements.
3. Data Analysis and Visualization: Elasticsearch provides a powerful query language and aggregation framework that enables complex analysis of data. Kibana, a data visualization tool, can be used to create interactive dashboards and visualizations of the data.
4. Search and Retrieval: Elasticsearch provides full-text search capabilities that enable users to search and retrieve data in real-time. This is especially useful in applications such as e-commerce, content management systems, and search engines.
5. Machine Learning: Elasticsearch also provides machine learning capabilities that enable the detection of anomalies and trends in data. This is useful in applications such as fraud detection, predictive maintenance, and customer segmentation.

Here is an example Python code snippet for indexing data in Elasticsearch using the Elasticsearch Python library:
```python
from elasticsearch import Elasticsearch

es = Elasticsearch()

doc = {
    'author': 'John Doe',
    'text': 'Elasticsearch is a powerful search engine',
    'timestamp': datetime.now(),
}

res = es.index(index='my-index', doc_type='tweet', id=1, body=doc)
print(res['result'])
```
In this example, a document containing author, text, and timestamp fields is indexed into an Elasticsearch index called `my-index`. The document type is set to `tweet`, and the document ID is set to 1. The `res` variable contains the indexing response, which can be used to verify that the document was successfully indexed.

Overall, Elasticsearch is a powerful tool that can be used in various applications in a data pipeline. It provides fast and scalable search and analytics capabilities, and its integration with other tools such as Kibana and Logstash makes it a popular choice for data processing and analysis.
What is inverse indexing

Inverse indexing, also known as inverted indexing, is a data structure used in search engines to enable fast full-text searches over large collections of documents. It is a technique that takes advantage of the structure of natural language text data to speed up search queries.

In inverse indexing, the system creates an index of all the unique words in a document collection, along with a list of the documents that contain each word. This index is then used to quickly locate documents that contain specific words or combinations of words.

For example, if we have a collection of documents containing text data, we can create an inverted index that lists all the unique words in the collection and the documents that contain each word. When a user enters a search query, the system looks up the words in the query in the index and returns a list of documents that contain those words.

Inverse indexing is an important technique for search engines and other applications that involve searching large collections of text data. It is used in many popular search engines, such as Google, as well as in other applications, such as e-commerce websites and social media platforms.

what's the difference between application layer and infrastructure layer

The application layer is the topmost layer in a computer network's protocol stack, responsible for providing services and interfaces to end-users and applications. It includes protocols such as HTTP, SMTP, and FTP.

The infrastructure layer is the lower layer in a network's protocol stack, responsible for providing the underlying transport and communication mechanisms required for data to be transmitted between networked devices. It includes protocols such as TCP/IP, Ethernet, and Wi-Fi.

In short, the application layer deals with user-facing aspects of network communication, while the infrastructure layer deals with the lower-level aspects of networking, such as routing and addressing.

why are these companies called "infrastructure companies"? why aren't other companies called infrastructure companies

Companies that are commonly referred to as "infrastructure companies" are those that provide the fundamental systems and services that are necessary for the functioning of a society or industry. These companies typically provide products and services related to transportation, energy, telecommunications, construction, and other essential systems that enable economic activity and daily life.

These companies are considered infrastructure companies because they build, operate, and maintain the foundational systems that support the economy and society. For example, companies that provide energy and power are essential for powering homes and businesses, while transportation companies enable the movement of people and goods. Telecommunications companies provide the connectivity required for communication, while construction companies build and maintain buildings, roads, and other physical infrastructure.

Other companies that are not traditionally considered infrastructure companies are typically not involved in the provision of essential services and systems that support the functioning of a society or industry. While these companies may provide important products and services, they are not typically viewed as infrastructure companies because they are not foundational to the economy and society.
what defines an essential service

An essential service is a service that is necessary for the well-being of society, particularly during a crisis or emergency situation. The exact definition of an essential service may vary depending on the context and the society in question, but generally, it refers to services that are critical for the health, safety, and security of the population.

Essential services may include healthcare, emergency services, food and agriculture, water and wastewater, energy and utilities, transportation, and communication services. These services are typically considered essential because they are necessary for the basic functioning of society, particularly during a crisis or emergency situation.

In some cases, governments may also define essential services based on legal or regulatory requirements. For example, during the COVID-19 pandemic, many governments around the world have defined essential services in order to determine which businesses and activities should be allowed to continue operating during lockdowns and other restrictions.
is salesforce considered infrastructure?

Salesforce is primarily a customer relationship management (CRM) software company and is not typically considered an infrastructure company. While Salesforce provides a cloud-based platform for managing customer relationships and automating business processes, its products and services are focused on the application layer of the network stack, rather than the lower-level infrastructure layer.

However, Salesforce does provide infrastructure-related services through its cloud platform, including data storage and processing, application development and hosting, and various integration services. These infrastructure services are designed to support the deployment and operation of Salesforce applications and other cloud-based services, but they are not the primary focus of the company. Therefore, while Salesforce does provide some infrastructure-related services, it is primarily a software and application provider rather than an infrastructure provider.

Docker provides a set of software tools for building, distributing, and running containerized applications. Containers are a lightweight form of virtualization that allows applications to run in isolated environments, making it easier to package and deploy applications across different computing environments. Docker provides the underlying technology and infrastructure that makes containerization possible, including the Docker Engine, which is a runtime environment for containers, and Docker Hub, which is a cloud-based registry for storing and sharing container images.

While Docker also provides some application-level functionality, such as Docker Compose for defining and running multi-container applications, its primary focus is on infrastructure-level technology that enables application development and deployment.

To improve a localization operation for a research and discovery and information services SaaS platform, technology and ML/AI can play a significant role. Here are some ways technology and ML/AI can enhance the building blocks mentioned earlier:

1. Localization strategy: Use data analytics and ML/AI to identify the most relevant languages and regions to target based on user behavior and engagement data. This can help you prioritize your localization efforts and improve ROI.
2. Localization team: Use ML/AI to automate repetitive and time-consuming tasks like translation, review, and quality assurance. This can free up your team's time to focus on more strategic tasks that require human expertise.
3. Content management system: Use ML/AI to automatically detect and segment content for translation, and to suggest translations based on context and previous translations. This can speed up the translation process and reduce errors.
4. Translation memory: Use ML/AI to automatically update and improve the translation memory as new content is translated. This can improve the accuracy and consistency of translations over time.
5. Machine translation: Use ML/AI to train machine translation models specific to your industry and content types. This can improve the accuracy and appropriateness of machine-generated translations.
6. Quality assurance: Use ML/AI to automatically detect and flag potential translation errors and inconsistencies. This can help improve the quality of translations and reduce the need for manual quality checks.
7. Continuous improvement: Use ML/AI to analyze translation data and identify trends and areas for improvement. This can help you optimize your localization process over time.

To mature the localization operation using technology and ML/AI, consider:

1. Implementing more advanced ML/AI models: As you collect more data and improve your ML/AI capabilities, consider implementing more advanced models that can handle more complex tasks like sentiment analysis and content personalization.
2. Integrating with other systems: Integrate your localization systems with other systems like customer relationship management (CRM) and content management systems (CMS) to streamline workflows and improve efficiency.
3. Building a knowledge base: Use ML/AI to build a knowledge base of common translation issues and solutions, which can help improve consistency and reduce errors over time.
4. Augmenting human expertise: Use ML/AI to augment the expertise of your localization team, providing them with insights and recommendations to improve the quality and relevance of translations.
5. Investing in specialized technology: Consider investing in specialized technology like speech recognition and natural language processing (NLP) to handle more complex localization tasks like audio and video content localization.

By leveraging technology and ML/AI, a localization operation can become more efficient, accurate, and effective in delivering quality translations at scale.

Explain how Facebook automatic translations works: localization strategy, teams, TMS, MT, TM, standards and guidelines, I18N tools, methods, models, workflows, technics, vendors. Put in table format and include diagrams

Sure! Here's an overview of how Facebook's automatic translation process works:

| Component | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Localization strategy | Facebook's localization strategy is centered around providing a personalized user experience. The platform uses data analytics to identify which languages and regions to target, and prioritizes translations based on user behavior and engagement. |
| Localization team | Facebook's localization team is made up of both in-house and external translators, as well as project managers and quality assurance specialists. |
| Translation management system (TMS) | Facebook uses a proprietary TMS to manage translation workflows, which includes features such as automatic content extraction, translation memory, and quality assurance checks. |
| Machine translation (MT) | Facebook uses a combination of rule-based and statistical MT models to provide initial translations. These models are trained on large amounts of data and continually updated to improve accuracy. |
| Translation memory (TM) | Facebook's TMS includes a translation memory that stores previously translated content and suggests translations for new content. |
| Standards and guidelines | Facebook has a set of localization standards and guidelines that govern things like tone, style, and terminology. These guidelines are used to ensure consistency across translations. |
| Internationalization (I18N) tools | Facebook has a range of I18N tools that help developers identify and fix potential localization issues in code, such as hard-coded strings or language-specific formatting. |
| Methods | Facebook uses a mix of human and machine translation to provide translations quickly and accurately. The platform also uses crowdsourcing to help validate translations and gather feedback from users. |
| Models | Facebook's MT models are based on both statistical and rule-based approaches, and incorporate neural machine translation (NMT) for improved accuracy. The platform also uses contextual models to improve translations for specific content types or user segments. |
| Workflows | Facebook's translation workflows involve content extraction, translation by either human or machine translation, quality assurance, and deployment. The platform uses automation and machine learning to streamline these workflows and improve efficiency. |
| Technics | Facebook uses a range of NLP and machine learning techniques to improve the accuracy and relevance of translations. These include context-aware translation, dynamic glossary creation, and deep learning models for more accurate MT. |
| Vendors | Facebook works with a range of external vendors to provide human translation services and support for its localization efforts. |

Here's a diagram to illustrate the different components of Facebook's automatic translation process:

Overall, Facebook's automatic translation process combines both human and machine translation to provide fast and accurate translations at scale. The platform also leverages advanced machine learning techniques to improve the accuracy and relevance of its translations over time.

Title:
Life Coach
Logline:
"He can't get his own life together, but he'll fix yours!"
"Can he fix your life? Absolutely. Can he fix his own? Not a chance."
"Can a life coach who can't get his own life in order help others?"
"He's a life coach who can't get his own life together, but somehow he helps everyone else."

Genre & Tone:
Comedy; Lighthearted, Quirky, and Heartwarming; "The Office," "Parks and Recreation"
Season 1 Plot Arc:
Our main character, a life coach, struggles to balance his own life while helping others through their own issues.
Key Characters:
Zack
Zack is a life coach who's always looking for ways to improve himself, even though his own life is often in disarray. He's a well-intentioned guy with a heart of gold, but his many flaws and quirks sometimes get in the way of his success. Zack is in his late thirties and has been through a lot of personal and professional setbacks. He's been divorced for a few years, but he's still hung up on his ex-wife and the life he used to have. He lives in the basement of his ex-wife's house, which can be both a blessing and a curse.
On one hand, he's still close to his two kids and is a great father to them. On the other hand, his living situation is far from ideal, and he often feels like a failure because he's not living the life he thought he would. Despite his struggles, Zack is a deeply caring and empathetic person, which is why he's drawn to the field of life coaching. He genuinely wants to help people and make a positive impact in their lives.
Zack is also incredibly well-read and has an encyclopedic knowledge of self-help books and programs. He's been through countless ten-step programs and is always looking for new ways to improve himself. This gives him a lot of material to work with as a life coach, but it also makes him a bit of a know-it-all at times. He can come across as preachy or insincere when he's spouting off advice that he's memorized from a book.
Despite his flaws, Zack is a lovable and endearing character who's easy to root for. He's always striving to be better, even if he's not always successful, and his heart is always in the right place.

Piper
Piper is a 16-year-old girl who is very precocious and intelligent. She's always reading books and asking deep philosophical questions that sometimes even stump her father. She's a bit of an over-achiever and is involved in many extracurricular activities, such as robotics club and debate team. Despite her mature demeanor, she still has moments of childlike wonder and silliness, especially when she's around her younger brother Caleb..
Piper is a bit of a perfectionist and can be hard on herself when things don't go according to plan. She's also very empathetic and has a strong sense of justice, often getting involved in social causes at school. Despite her busy schedule, she always finds time to help her father with his life coaching clients, often providing insightful observations and feedback.
However, Piper is also a bit of a neat freak and can be a bit bossy at times, which sometimes leads to conflicts with her more laid-back younger brother Max. She also struggles with anxiety and often worries about the future, especially with her parents' recent divorce. Overall, Piper is a well-rounded and complex character who adds depth and dimension to the show.
Caleb
Caleb is a 13-year-old boy who's the eldest of the two children that Zack has with his ex-wife. He's a bright and curious kid who loves playing video games and tinkering with electronics. Caleb is also very interested in self-improvement and personal growth, which he's learned from his dad's many failed attempts at being a life coach. Caleb often reads his dad's self-help books and tries to apply the lessons to his own life, with mixed results.
Despite being a bit of a tech geek, Caleb is also an avid outdoorsman and enjoys camping and hiking with his dad. He's a bit of an introvert, but he's not afraid to speak his mind when he feels strongly about something. Caleb's biggest challenge is dealing with the divorce of his parents and navigating the tension between his mom and dad. He often serves as a mediator between the two, which can be stressful for a young kid. Nonetheless, he tries to stay optimistic and positive, even in tough situations.
Audrey
Zack's ex-wife, Audrey, is a high-powered lawyer who takes her career very seriously. She's always been driven and ambitious, and this sometimes put a strain on their relationship. Despite their differences, Audrey and Zack tried to make it work for the sake of their children, but eventually decided to divorce.
Audrey can be very intense and demanding, and she doesn't have a lot of patience for people who don't share her work ethic. She's not afraid to speak her mind, and sometimes her bluntness can come across as harsh or even rude. However, deep down she cares deeply for her children and wants what's best for them.
Although she's moved on and remarried, Audrey still has some lingering feelings for Zack. She knows he's a good father and respects his dedication to his work, but she can't help but feel frustrated by his lack of progress in his personal life. Sometimes she wishes he would just grow up and take responsibility for his own happiness.
Henry
Audrey's new husband, Henry, is a successful real estate developer who is very practical and business-minded. He has a no-nonsense attitude and doesn't have much patience for Zack's eccentricities, but he tolerates him for the sake of his stepchildren. Henry is in his late 40s and is always dressed impeccably in designer suits. He's a bit of a control freak and likes everything to be in order, which is the complete opposite of Zack's chaotic lifestyle. Despite their differences, Henry respects Zack's abilities as a father and his desire to help others, but he's not sure what to make of Zack's attempts to become a self-help guru. He's skeptical of the self-help industry as a whole and thinks that people should just take responsibility for their own lives instead of relying on books and seminars. However, he doesn't want to upset Audrey or the kids, so he keeps his opinions to himself and tries to keep the peace.

Maddie
Madison "Maddie" Steele - A savvy and ambitious young entrepreneur who runs a self-help publishing company. Maddie sees the potential in Zack as a life coach and wants to help him monetize his skills by turning him into a guru and publishing his own self-help book. Maddie is always thinking about the next big thing and isn't afraid to take risks to make it happen. She's also a bit of a workaholic and can come off as pushy, but deep down she just wants to help Zack and make a name for herself in the industry. She's obsessed with her pet turtle, Sheldon, and often brings him to work with her.
Maya Patel 
A free-spirited, adventurous woman in her mid-thirties. She and Zack have been friends since college and have always shared a deep connection. Maya is a travel blogger and a yoga instructor, and she's always up for trying new things and pushing Zack out of his comfort zone. Despite being very different from Zack, Maya has a knack for understanding him and helping him through his struggles. She's also fiercely loyal and always there for Zack when he needs her, even if it means dropping everything to fly across the world at a moment's notice. She has a quirky habit of collecting snow globes from every place she visits, and has over 200 in her collection

Original Twist:
Despite his many flaws, the life coach's advice from the many 12-step programs he’s been through gives leads him to unexpected success in his new career.
Life Coach's brilliant advice comes from the many different 12-step programs he's gone through, resulting in a unique and unconventional approach to coaching.
Why it will succeed:
The show combines the relatable struggles of co-parenting and starting over with the wacky humor of a classic sitcom.
The unique premise of a life coach who can't seem to get his own life together offers endless comedic potential.
The show's blend of heart and humor, paired with a cast of lovable misfits, is sure to capture audiences' attention and keep them coming back for more.
The show's relatable characters and situations will resonate with viewers who have experienced divorce or blended families.
Our main character is relatable and endearing, despite his flaws. Life Coach is an endearing character with a heart of gold, making him relatable and beloved to audiences
The relatable and endearing nature of our main character will make viewers fall in love with him, flaws and all.
The show's unique take on the self-help industry will provide a fresh and entertaining perspective on a familiar topic.
idea for the first episode, Zack arrives back from his spiritual awakening from living with Monks in Nepal and wants to become a lifecoach to finally put all of his 12 step programs and self-help books to use. His ex-wife agrees he can live in the basement temporarily because the kids want him there. when he is able to mitigate some tension between Audrey and Henry using his self-help techniques (which shows them all that he might actually be a good life coach) they agree to let him stay to be closer to the kids until he's able to get his life coach business up and make enough money to get his own place

idea for the second episode, Zack finds a $20 check from grandma twenty years ago in an old birthday card he found while cleaning the basement so he can live there. He goes to the bank to cash it and two bank robbers in masks and guns take everyone hostage. Using his empathy and lifecoach techniques, he is able to get the robbers to give themselves up which is all caught by the tv cameras. the second episode ends with Zack going viral on YouTube and he says he is a life coach. His son builds him a website and Zack starts getting requests for life coaching

idea for third episode, the local college basketball coach emails Zack a request to come speak to the team who is underachieving right now. There he meets Maddie, the team's marketing director who has her own plans for Zack (to make money off of him). Zack tries to help each player individually but his advice backfires and the team splits apart even more. In the last game of the season, the team will make the post-season if they win. After dealing with his kids about something, Zack has a personal epiphany about where he failed as a father. It's then he realizes he needs to help the coach, not the players. His advice to the coach works because the coach is able to get the team to play better and win the game. Zack is now a hero in the town.

Rewrite this for brevity, in outline form:

![](https://miro.medium.com/max/875/1\*P6kOmMkdlQUUtKHcDXL1Sw.jpeg)

# Introduction

Google BigQuery is an enterprise data warehouse built using BigTable and Google Cloud Platform. It’s serverless and completely managed. BigQuery works great with all sizes of data, from a 100 row Excel spreadsheet to several Petabytes of data. Most importantly, it can execute a complex query on those data within a few seconds.

We need to note before we proceed, BigQuery is not a transactional database. It takes around 2 seconds to run a simple query like ‘SELECT \\* FROM bigquery-public-data.object LIMIT 10’ on a 100 KB table with 500 rows. Hence, it shouldn’t be thought of as OLTP (Online Transaction Processing) database. BigQuery is for Big Data!

BigQuery supports SQL-like query, which makes it user-friendly and beginner friendly. It’s accessible via its web UI, command-line tool, or client library (written in C#, Go, Java, Node.js, PHP, Python, and Ruby). You can also take advantage of its REST APIs and get our job\` done by sending a JSON request.

Now, let’s dive deeper to understand it better. Suppose you are a data scientist (or a startup which analyzes data) and you need to analyze terabytes of data. If you choose a tool like MySQL, the first step before even thinking about any query is to have an infrastructure in place, that can store this magnitude of data.

Designing this setup itself will be a difficult task because you have to figure out what will be the RAM size, DCOS or Kubernetes, and other factors. And if you have streaming data coming, you will need to set up and maintain a Kafka cluster. In BigQuery, all you have to do is a bulk upload of your CSV/JSON file, and you are done. BigQuery handles all the backend for you. If you need streaming data ingestion, you can use Fluentd. Another advantage of this is that you can connect Google Analytics with BigQuery seamlessly.

BigQuery is serverless, highly available, and petabyte scalable service which allows you to execute complex SQL queries quickly. It lets you focus on analysis rather than handling infrastructure. The idea of hardware is completely abstracted and not visible to us, not even as virtual machines.

# The Architecture of Google BigQuery

You don’t need to know too much about the underlying architecture of BigQuery. That’s actually the whole idea of it — you don’t need to worry about architecture and operation.

However, understanding BigQuery Architecture helps us in controlling costs, optimizing query performance, and optimizing storage. BigQuery is built using the Google Dremel paper.

\*\*Quoting an Abstract from the Google Dremel Paper -\*\*

\*“Dremel is a scalable, interactive ad-hoc query system for analysis of read-only nested data. By combining multi-level execution trees and columnar data layout, it is capable of running aggregation queries over trillion-row tables in seconds. The system scales to thousands of CPUs and petabytes of data and has thousands of users at Google. In this paper, we describe the architecture and implementation of Dremel and explain how it complements MapReduce-based computing. We present a novel columnar storage representation for nested records and discuss experiments on few-thousand node instances of the system.”\*

You can read the paper here — .

Dremel was in production at Google since 2006. Google used it for the following tasks -

\* Analysis of crawled web documents.
\* Tracking install data for applications on Android Market.
\* Crash reporting for Google products.
\* OCR results from Google Books.
\* Spam analysis.
\* Debugging of map tiles on Google Maps.
\* Tablet migrations in managed Bigtable instances.
\* Results of tests run on Google’s distributed build system.
\* Disk I/O statistics for hundreds of thousands of disks.
\* Resource monitoring for jobs run in Google’s data centers.
\* Symbols and dependencies in Google’s codebase.

BigQuery is much more than Dremel. Dremel is just a query execution engine, whereas Bigquery is based on interesting technologies like Borg (predecessor of Kubernetes) and Colossus. Colossus is the successor to the Google File System (GFS) as mentioned in Google Spanner Paper.

![](https://miro.medium.com/max/751/1\*oFbNKWOs-9CrLaprmTDfKA.png)

# How BigQuery Stores Data?

BigQuery stores data in a columnar format — Capacitor (which is a successor of ColumnarIO). BigQuery achieves very high compression ratio and scan throughput. Unlike ColumnarIO, now on BigQuery, you can directly operate on compressed data without decompressing it.

Columnar storage has the following advantages:

\* \*\*Traffic minimization\*\*— When you submit a query, the required column values on each query are scanned and only those are transferred on query execution. E.g., a query \`SELECT title FROM Collection\` would access the title column values only.
\* \*\*Higher compression ratio\*\* — Columnar storage can achieve a compression ratio of 1:10, whereas ordinary row-based storage can compress at roughly 1:3.

![](https://miro.medium.com/max/868/1\*rmmUUcF-pno3IE3iq5X6Yw.png)

\*(Image source: Google Dremel Paper)\*

Columnar storage has the disadvantage of not working efficiently when updating existing records. That is why Dremel doesn’t support any update queries.

# How the Query Gets Executed?

BigQuery depends on Borg for data processing. Borg simultaneously instantiates hundreds of Dremel jobs across required clusters made up of thousands of machines. In addition to assigning compute capacity for Dremel jobs, Borg handles fault-tolerance as well.

Now, how do you design/execute a query which can run on thousands of nodes and fetches the result? This challenge was overcome by using the Tree Architecture. This architecture forms a gigantically parallel distributed tree for pushing down a query to the tree and aggregating the results from the leaves at a blazingly fast speed.

![](https://miro.medium.com/max/829/1\*FwBrz2pdhmoSlBzgQjuKWg.png)

\*(Image source: Google Dremel Paper)\*

# BigQuery vs. MapReduce

The key differences between BigQuery and MapReduce are -

\* Dremel is designed as an interactive data analysis tool for large datasets
\* MapReduce is designed as a programming framework to batch process large datasets

Moreover, Dremel finishes most queries within seconds or tens of seconds and can even be used by non-programmers, whereas MapReduce takes much longer (sometimes even hours or days) to process a query.

Following is a comparison on running MapReduce on a row and columnar DB:

![](https://miro.medium.com/max/875/1\*jEPPPfwnJNuivXzXTDZFyQ.png)

\*(Image source: Google Dremel Paper)\*

Another important thing to note is that BigQuery is meant to analyze structured data (SQL) but in MapReduce, you can write logic for unstructured data as well.

# Comparing BigQuery and Redshift

In Redshift, you need to allocate different instance types and create your own clusters. The benefit of this is that it lets you tune the compute/storage to meet your needs. However, you have to be aware of (virtualized) hardware limits and scale up/out based on that. Note that you are charged by the hour for each instance you spin up.

In BigQuery, you just upload the data and query it. It is a truly managed service. You are charged by storage, streaming inserts, and queries.

There are more similarities in both the data warehouses than the differences.

A smart user will definitely take advantage of the hybrid cloud (GCE+AWS) and leverage different services offered by both the ecosystems. Check out here.

# Getting Started With Google BigQuery

Following is a quick example to show how you can quickly get started with BigQuery:

1. There are many public datasets available on bigquery, you are going to play with ‘bigquery-public-data:stackoverflow’ dataset. You can click on the “Add Data” button on the left panel and select datasets.

![](https://miro.medium.com/max/875/1\*dRFs1aA3zT3pBC4vc5RE5A.png)

2\. Next, find a language which has the best community, based on the response time. You can write the following query to do that.

3\. Now you can execute the query and get results -

![](https://miro.medium.com/max/875/1\*igz21iSrnJ\_jpBESK6v9wA.png)

You can see that C has the best community followed by javascript!

![](https://miro.medium.com/max/448/1\*Rt5pJfV9OQtaKfaHKkZh8w.png)

# How to do Machine Learning on BigQuery?

Now that you have a sound understanding of BigQuery. It’s time for some real action.

As discussed above, you can connect Google Analytics with BigQuery by going to the Google Analytics Admin panel, then enable BigQuery by clicking on PROPERTY column, click All Products, then click Link BigQuery. After that, you need to enter BigQuery ID (or project number) and then BigQuery will be linked to Google Analytics. \*Note — Right now BigQuery integration is only available to Google Analytics 360\*.

Assuming that you already have uploaded your google analytics data, here is how you can create a logistic regression model. Here, you are predicting whether a website visitor will make a transaction or not.

Create a model named ‘velotio\\_tutorial.sample\\_model’. Now set the ‘model\\_type’ as ‘logistic\\_reg’ because you want to train a logistic regression model. A logistic regression model splits input data into two classes and gives the probability that the data is in one of the classes. Usually, in “spam or not spam” type of problems, you use logistic regression. Here, the problem is similar — a transaction will be made or not.

The above query gets the total number of page views, the country from where the session originated, the operating system of visitors device, the total number of e-commerce transactions within the session, etc.

Now you just press run query to execute the query.

# Conclusion

BigQuery is a query service that allows us to run SQL-like queries against multiple terabytes of data in a matter of seconds. If you have structured data, BigQuery is the best option to go for. It can help even a non-programmer to get the analytics right! Related Read — .

write a very short summary of 5 lines from this text: Introduction to Bookipedia

Welcome to "The Future of Reading: How Bookipedia is Changing the Way We Use Books." In this presentation, we will be introducing the concept of Bookipedia and discussing how it is revolutionising the way we experience literature. Bookipedia is a revolutionary platform that uses AI to enable communication with books and enhance the reading experience. With its ability to analyze the text and context of a book and generate responses to reader questions, Bookipedia offers a more interactive and personalised reading experience. This has the potential to change the way people use books and experience literature, making reading a more interactive and engaging activity. We hope that this presentation will give you a better understanding of Bookipedia and its capabilities, and encourage you to try it out for yourself and experience the future of reading.

The Limitations of Traditional Reading

One of the main limitations of traditional reading is that it is a one-sided activity. When we read a book, we absorb the information contained within the pages, but we have no way to gauge our understanding or ask for clarification. This can lead to confusion or misunderstandings, and can make it difficult to fully engage with the material. Traditional reading is also a solitary activity, which can make it harder to connect with others who have read the same book or to discuss and explore the ideas contained within it. These limitations can make reading less enjoyable and less effective as a learning tool. Bookipedia aims to overcome these limitations by providing a more interactive and personalised reading experience, enabling readers to ask questions, seek clarification, and engage in a dialogue with the book and with other readers.

The Power of AI in Reading

Artificial intelligence has the power to transform many aspects of our lives, and reading is no exception. AI has the ability to analyse the text and context of a book and generate responses to reader questions, allowing for a more interactive and personalised reading experience. This can help to improve comprehension and make the reading experience more engaging and enjoyable.
